para.text: The Parliamentary Counsel Office has made editorial and format changes to this version using the powers under subpart 2 of Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019.
Note 4 at the end of this version provides a list of the amendments included in it.

cover.reprint-note.admin-office: This Act is administered by the New Zealand Defence Force.

front.enactment: The Parliament of New Zealand enacts as follows:

prov.heading: Title

para.text: This Act is the Veterans' Support Act 2014.

prov.heading: Commencement

para.text: This Act (except the provisions specified in subsections (2) and (3)) comes into force on the day that is 4 months after the date on which this Act receives the Royal assent.
Sections 22, 23, subparts 1 and 2 of Part 8, and subpart 2 of Part 9 come into force on the day after the date on which this Act receives the Royal assent.
The following provisions come into force on the day that is 16 months after the date on which this Act receives the Royal assent:
Part 4 (Scheme Two):
subpart 4 of Part 5:
Schedule 2 (except Part 1).

prov.heading: Purpose

para.text: The purpose of this Act is to provide for—
the rehabilitation of and support for veterans who, as a result of being placed in harm's way in the service of New Zealand, have been injured or become ill; and
entitlements for eligible veterans who suffer service-related injuries or illnesses; and

entitlements and support for eligible spouses, partners, children, and dependants of veterans with service-related injuries or illnesses and for other persons who provide non-professional support to those veterans.

The purpose of Part 2 is to promote positive veteran and claimant interaction with Veterans' Affairs New Zealand through the development and operation of a Code of Veterans' and Other Claimants' Rights.

history.history-note: Section 3(1)﻿(c): replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 4 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Overview

para.text: This Act comprises 9 Parts.
This Part—
sets out the purpose of this Act:
provides that this Act binds the Crown:
defines terms used in this Act:
specifies general principles about how functions and powers under the Act are to be performed and exercised:
states VANZ's general duties:
provides the process for how claims are to be decided and provides certain presumptions relating to fitness for service, the cause of injury or illness, and paired organs:
provides for the indexation of certain entitlements.
Part 2 provides for a Code of Veterans' and Other Claimants' Rights.

Part 3 (Scheme One) provides entitlements to veterans whose injury or illness is caused by, contributed to by, or aggravated by qualifying service before 1 April 1974 and their spouses, partners, children, and dependants. Certain veterans with qualifying service after 1 April 1974 are also included.
The entitlements are—
travel costs associated with treatment (subpart 2); and
the disablement pension (subpart 3); and
weekly income compensation (subpart 4); and
entitlements for spouses, partners, children, and dependants of veterans (subpart 5); and
children's bursaries (subpart 6).

Part 4 (Scheme Two) provides entitlements to veterans whose injury or illness is caused by, contributed to by, or aggravated by qualifying operational service on or after 1 April 1974 and to their spouses, partners, children, and dependants. Certain veterans with qualifying operational service after 1 April 1974 who are covered by Part 3 (Scheme One) are excluded.
The entitlements are—
vocational services for spouses or partners (subpart 3); and
payments for permanent impairment (subpart 4); and
weekly compensation (subpart 5).
Part 5 provides additional entitlements to or in respect of veterans to whom Scheme One or Scheme Two applies.
The entitlements are—
treatment (subpart 2); and
rehabilitation for veterans (subpart 3 and Schedule 2); and
participation in the veterans' independence programme (subpart 4); and
aids and appliances (subpart 5); and
motor vehicle grants (subpart 6); and
retirement lump sum (subpart 7); and
funeral expenses (subpart 8).
Part 6 provides an entitlement to a veteran's pension to certain veterans and their spouses and partners who have reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age.
Part 7 provides for—
the establishment and functions of VANZ (subpart 1):
the review of decisions by VANZ by a review officer or review panel, and for the establishment of a Veterans' Service Review Panel (subpart 2):
appeals against a review decision to an appeal board and for the establishment of a Veterans' Entitlements Appeal Board (also subpart 2).
Part 8 provides for—
the establishment and functions of the Veterans' Advisory Board (subpart 1):
the establishment and functions of the Veterans' Health Advisory Panel (subpart 2):
the administration of the Veterans' Medical Research Trust Fund (subpart 3).
Part 9 provides for a range of miscellaneous matters as follows:
notification of documents and information (subpart 1):
regulations (subpart 2):
offences (subpart 3):
repeals, transitional provisions, and consequential amendments (subpart 4):
other matters relating to missing veterans, arrangements with other countries, and exclusion of Crown liability for the death or disablement of veterans (subpart 5).
This section—
is only a guide to the general scheme and effect of this Act; and
does not limit or affect the other provisions of this Act.

prov.label: 4A
prov.heading: Transitional, savings, and related provisions

para.text: The transitional, savings, and related provisions set out in Schedule 1 have effect according to their terms.

history.history-note: Section 4A: inserted, on 21 January 2019, by section 9 of the Social Assistance (Residency Qualification) Legislation Act 2018 (2018 No 45).

prov.heading: Act to bind the Crown

para.text: This Act binds the Crown.

prov.heading: Application of provisions of this Act

history.history-note: Section 6: repealed, on 21 January 2019, by section 10 of the Social Assistance (Residency Qualification) Legislation Act 2018 (2018 No 45).

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,—
accepted late-onset condition, in relation to Part 4 (Scheme Two),—
means a condition specified in regulations made under section 265 as accepted as being linked to specific exposure or specific events occurring during qualifying operational service; and
without limiting paragraph (a), includes conditions specified in regulations referred to in that paragraph, being—
a malignancy or other physical disorder that is capable of being caused by exposure during qualifying operational service that occurs 10 or more years before the onset of the illness; or
a mental disorder that is capable of being caused by an event that occurs 10 or more years before the onset of the illness; and

a mental disorder that is capable of being caused by an event that occurs less than 10 years before the onset of the illness and that is capable of causing the person suffering from it to be unable to seek medical help for the disorder during that period
advisory board means the Veterans' Advisory Board established under Part 8
advisory panel means the Veterans' Health Advisory Panel established under Part 8
appeal board means the Veterans' Entitlements Appeal Board established under Part 7
armed forces has the same meaning as in section 2(1) of the Defence Act 1990
child, in relation to a veteran,—
means a natural child of the veteran; and
includes—
an adopted child of the veteran; and
a child of whom the veteran is or has been a guardian; and
a grandchild or a whāngai of the veteran in relation to whom the veteran acts or has acted as a parent or a guardian; and
includes any other child who would ordinarily be regarded as a child of the veteran because the veteran—
is or has been the spouse or partner of one of the child's parents; and
acts or has acted as a parent of the child
Code—
means the Code of Veterans' and Other Claimants' Rights approved under section 34; and
includes any amendments to the Code approved under section 37
counselling means counselling that is provided by—
a health practitioner under the Health Practitioners Competence Assurance Act 2003 and that is within the practitioner’s scope of practice under that Act; or
a person who is recognised by the Accident Compensation Corporation as a counsellor for the purposes of the Accident Compensation Act 2001
Defence Force has the same meaning as in section 2(1) of the Defence Act 1990
dependant, in relation to a veteran, means any of the following persons:
a person (not being the spouse, partner, or child of the veteran) who—
is under 18 years of age; and
is wholly or primarily dependent on the veteran for financial support; and
ordinarily resides with the veteran:
a person (not being the spouse, partner, or child of the veteran) who—
is 18 years of age or more; and
is under the care of the veteran; and
ordinarily resides with the veteran because the person is unable to live independently of the veteran due to disability, illness, or advanced age:
a person who—
is a child of the veteran; and
is 18 years of age or more; and
is under the care of the veteran; and
is unable to live independently of the veteran due to physical or mental infirmity
deputy general manager means the Deputy General Manager of VANZ appointed under section 200
full-time, in relation to employment and work, means employment for either—
an average of at least 30 hours per week; or

a lesser number of hours, if the lesser number of hours is defined as full-time employment in the employment agreement under which the veteran was employed, because of the particular nature of that employment
full-time study has the same meaning as in section 6(1) of the Accident Compensation Act 2001
general manager means the General Manager of VANZ appointed under section 200
guardian has the same meaning as in section 15 of the Care of Children Act 2004
health practitioner has the same meaning as in section 5(1) of the Health Practitioners Competence Assurance Act 2003
home—
means residential premises in which the veteran lives and that are owned, rented, or otherwise lawfully occupied by the veteran or the veteran’s parent, guardian, spouse, or partner; and

includes residential premises in which the veteran proposes to live after they are built and that will be owned, rented, or otherwise lawfully occupied by the veteran or the veteran’s parent, guardian, spouse, or partner; and
includes any other premises in which the veteran lives and for which the veteran is solely responsible for home maintenance; but
does not include any hotel or motel, or any hospital, hostel, rest home, or other institution
impairment means a loss or abnormality of psychological, physiological, or anatomical function or structure
long-term residential care has the same meaning as in section 11 of the Residential Care and Disability Support Services Act 2018

medical practitioner means a health practitioner who is, or is deemed to be, registered with the Medical Council of New Zealand continued by section 114(1)﻿(a) of the Health Practitioners Competence Assurance Act 2003 as a practitioner of the profession of medicine
member of the armed forces means a person who is or has been a member of a New Zealand armed force raised by the Governor-General on behalf of the Sovereign,—
whether in New Zealand or elsewhere; and
whether before or after the passing of this Act
Minister means the Minister of the Crown who is, with the authority of the Prime Minister, for the time being responsible for the administration of this Act
New Zealand superannuation qualification age means the age at which a person becomes entitled to receive New Zealand superannuation under the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001
other claimants means—

persons who have served as members of the armed forces before 1 April 1974 and whose injury or illness was caused by, contributed to by, or aggravated by routine service in New Zealand or overseas; and
spouses or partners of veterans; and
children of veterans; and
dependants of veterans; and
persons (other than the spouse or partner, or a child or dependant, of the veteran) who provide non-professional support to veterans for the purposes of section 107
partner means a civil union partner or a de facto partner

QES means the Quarterly Employment Survey of wages published by Statistics New Zealand or, if that survey ceases to be published, any measure certified by the Government Statistician as being equivalent to that survey
review decision means a decision made by a review officer, a review panel, or the general manager under subpart 2 of Part 7

review officer means a member or a contractor of the Defence Force appointed by the general manager (or by the Chief of Defence Force if section 219(2)﻿(b) applies) to conduct reviews under subpart 2 of Part 7
review panel means a Veterans' Service Review Panel established under section 223

service-related, in relation to an injury, an illness, a condition, or a whole-person impairment, means an injury, an illness, or a whole-person impairment caused by, contributed to by, or aggravated by qualifying service
service-related death,—
in relation to Part 3 (Scheme One), means death attributable to qualifying service; and
in relation to Part 4 (Scheme Two), means—
the death of a person who, at the time of the person's death, was taking part in qualifying operational service:
the death of a person within 10 years after the person took part in qualifying operational service from a service-related illness or injury:
the death of a person more than 10 years after the person took part in qualifying operational service from an accepted late-onset condition
treatment provider—
means a chiropractor, dentist, medical laboratory technologist, nurse, nurse practitioner, occupational therapist, optometrist, osteopath, physiotherapist, podiatrist, or medical practitioner; and

includes a member of any occupational group as added for the purposes of this definition by regulations made under section 265 and subject to any criteria specified in those regulations, including (but not limited to) whether and, if so, the extent to which members of an occupational group are recognised by the Accident Compensation Corporation as treatment providers for the purposes of the Accident Compensation Act 2001
VANZ means Veterans' Affairs New Zealand established under section 198
veteran means—
a member of the armed forces who took part in qualifying operational service at the direction of the New Zealand Government; or
a member of the armed forces who took part in qualifying routine service before 1 April 1974; or
a person—
who has been—
appointed as an employee of the Defence Force under section 61A of the Defence Act 1990; or
seconded to the Defence Force with the permission of the Chief of Defence Force; and
who took part in qualifying operational service at the direction of the New Zealand Government; or
a person who, immediately before the commencement of Part 3 of this Act, is eligible for a pension under the following provisions of the War Pensions Act 1954:
section 19 (but only if the person was a member of the forces):
section 55 or 56:
whāngai means a child adopted by the veteran in accordance with Māori custom
working day means a day of the week other than—
a Saturday, a Sunday, Waitangi Day, Good Friday, Easter Monday, Anzac Day, the Sovereign’s birthday, Te Rā Aro ki a Matariki/Matariki Observance Day, and Labour Day; and
if Waitangi Day or Anzac Day falls on a Saturday or a Sunday, the following Monday; and
the day observed in the appropriate area as the anniversary of the province of which the area forms a part; and
a day in the period commencing on 25 December in any year and ending with 15 January in the following year.

prov.heading: Meanings of different types of service

para.text: This section defines the different types of service referred to in this Act.
In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires,—
qualifying operational service means—
service on any deployment treated as a war or emergency for the purposes of the War Pensions Act 1954; or
service on any deployment declared to be operational service under section 9
qualifying routine service means service in the armed forces before 1 April 1974 that is not qualifying operational service
qualifying service means—
qualifying operational service; or
qualifying routine service.

history.history-note: Section 8: replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 6 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Declarations that deployments are operational service

para.text: As soon as practicable after a decision is made to deploy members of the Defence Force, the Chief of Defence Force must provide a report to the Minister that assesses the operational and environmental threats posed to the health and well-being of the members.

If further information on the threats posed to the members becomes available or the nature or significance of the threats changes, the Chief of Defence Force must provide a further report to the Minister.
If the Minister is satisfied that the deployment poses a significant risk of harm to the members deployed, the Minister must declare—
the deployment to be operational service; and
the area (whether land, sea, or airspace) in which the deployment is to be operational service; and
the period during which the deployment is to be operational service.

The Chief of Defence Force must advise the Minister when the deployment ends or the threats posed to members on which the Minister relied in making a declaration under subsection (3) escalate, abate, or cease.
The Minister may shorten or extend the period specified under subsection (3)﻿(c) if,—

in the case of shortening the period, the Minister is satisfied that the deployment has ceased or will cease to pose a significant risk of harm to members of the deployed force before the expiry of the period; and
in the case of extending the period, the Minister is satisfied that the deployment will continue to pose a significant risk of harm to members of the deployed force after the expiry of the period.
The power under subsection (3) includes the power to make a declaration in respect of a deployment that has ceased, whether the deployment began before or after the commencement of this Act.
In relation to any declaration referred to in subsection (5A),—
the reports referred to in subsections (1) and (2) may be provided at any time; and
subsection (3) applies with any necessary modifications; and
subsections (4) and (5) do not apply.

The Minister may extend the period specified under subsection (3)﻿(c) in a declaration referred to in subsection (5A) if the Minister is satisfied that the deployment continued to pose a significant risk of harm to members of the deployed force after the expiry of the period.
Subsection (5E) applies in respect of a declaration (a replacement declaration) that—
is in respect of a deployment that has ceased; and
the Minister is satisfied replaces another declaration under this section or a decision under section 70A or 80A of the War Pensions Act 1954 (the earlier declaration or decision).

Before making a replacement declaration, the Minister must be satisfied that support to which any particular person would, at any time (whenever arising), have been entitled under the Act as a result of the earlier declaration or decision will not be reduced as a result of the making of the replacement declaration.
VANZ must publish the making or amending of a declaration—
on an Internet site maintained by or on behalf of VANZ; and
in any other manner that VANZ thinks appropriate to bring the matter to the attention of claimants and the general public.
VANZ is not required to publish the making or amending of a declaration if satisfied that there are reasons of operational or national security not to publish.
For the purposes of this section,—
environmental threat means a threat posed by 1 or more of the following:
water and food quality or availability, or sanitation conditions:
epidemics and other diseases, including diseases that are endemic:
insect, plant, and animal hazards:
roads and associated traffic hazards:
any other matter that the Minister considers poses an environmental threat
operational threat means a threat posed by 1 or more of the following:
the activity of individuals, stakeholders and factions, security forces, and other groups, including those conducting military and political activity:
the local conditions and hazards, including violence connected with crime, civil disturbance, rioting, and protest action:
the risk associated with mines, unexploded ordnance, and booby traps:
the risk associated with chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear exposure:
acts of terrorism, whether or not directed at members of the Defence Force:
any other matter that the Minister considers poses an operational threat.

subpart.heading: General principles and related matters

prov.heading: Functions to be performed and powers to be exercised in accordance with certain principles

para.text: Every person who performs any function or exercises any power under this Act must do so—
in acknowledgement, on behalf of the community, of the responsibility for the injury, illness, or death of veterans as a result of them being placed in harm's way in the service of New Zealand; and
in accordance with the following principles:
the principle of providing fair entitlements to veterans and other claimants:
the principle of promoting equal treatment of equal claims:
the principle of taking a benevolent approach to claims:
the principle of determining claims—
in accordance with substantial justice and the merits of the claim; and
not in accordance with any technicalities, legal forms, or legal rules of evidence.

history.history-note: Section 10(b)﻿(i): replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 8 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Information to claimant

para.text: VANZ must keep a veteran or other claimant who makes a claim for an entitlement under this Act up to date on the progress of the claim.
Without limiting subsection (1), VANZ must,—
within 7 working days after receiving a claim, inform the veteran or other claimant that the claim has been received; and
notify the veteran or other claimant, in accordance with subsection (3), of VANZ’s decision on the claim.
Notification of a decision must—
be given as soon as practicable after VANZ makes the decision; and
be in writing; and
include the reason for the decision; and
inform the veteran or other claimant of—
their rights of review under subpart 2 of Part 7; and
in the case of a claim for a veteran’s pension, their right of appeal under section 175(1); and
be given in time to allow the veteran or other claimant to exercise those rights.

history.history-note: Section 11: replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 9 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Performance of functions and exercise of powers

para.text: VANZ must perform its functions and exercise its powers—
on reasonable grounds; and
in a timely manner.
In complying with subsection (1), VANZ must have regard to the following:
the requirements of this Act:
the nature of the function or power:
all the circumstances.
Without limiting subsection (1)﻿(b), VANZ must ensure that a decision on a claim for an entitlement under this Act is made as soon as practicable after receiving the claim.

history.history-note: Section 12: replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 9 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: VANZ must tell claimant about review rights

history.history-note: Section 13: repealed, on 1 October 2020, by section 9 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

subpart.crosshead: How claims are to be decided

prov.heading: Process for deciding claims

para.text: This section sets out the sequential steps to be taken in deciding whether a claim under this Act is accepted.
The first step is to—
consider all the available material that is relevant; and
decide whether the material is consistent with an hypothesis that the veteran’s injury, illness, or death was service-related.
If the material is consistent with an hypothesis as specified in subsection (2), the second step is to decide whether there is a statement of principles that applies.
If there is no statement of principles that applies, section 15 applies.
If there is a statement of principles that applies, the third step is to decide whether the hypothesis is consistent with the statement of principles.

If the hypothesis is consistent with the statement of principles, the fourth step is to accept the claim, unless there are reasonable grounds for believing that the veteran’s injury, illness, or death was not service-related.
In this section and section 15, statement of principles means a statement of principles that, under section 22(6) and regulations made under section 265, applies for the purposes of this Act.

prov.heading: Where no statement of principles applies

para.text: This section applies if, under section 14(4), there is no applicable statement of principles.
The person deciding whether to accept the claim must decide whether the claim is consistent with an hypothesis that is reasonable.

If the claim is consistent with an hypothesis that is reasonable, then the claim must be accepted, unless there are reasonable grounds for believing that the veteran's injury, illness, or death was not service-related.
An hypothesis is reasonable if it is—
more than a possibility; and
consistent with the known facts; and
not inconsistent with proved or known scientific facts.

prov.heading: Process in sections 14 and 15 subject to other sections

para.text: The process set out in sections 14 and 15 applies to the extent that it is not modified or overtaken by the presumptions in sections 17 to 21 or the provisions of section 28.

prov.heading: Presumption of physical and mental fitness for service

para.text: For the purposes of this Act, a person who is medically assessed by the Defence Force to be physically and mentally fit for service in the armed forces is presumed to be physically and mentally fit for service in the armed forces.
The presumption in subsection (1) is conclusive, but—
applies subject to subsection (3); and
does not apply if subsection (4) applies.
The presumption in subsection (1) applies subject to any physical or mental condition that—
qualifies a person's physical and mental fitness for service in the armed forces; and
is noted in writing and given to the person at the time the person's physical and mental fitness is assessed.
The presumption in subsection (1) does not apply if—
the person failed to disclose, after proper inquiry of the person, any material facts in relation to his or her physical or mental health or history of physical or mental health; or
by reason of any false statement, non-disclosure, or misrepresentation by the person, he or she was incorrectly assessed as physically and mentally fit for service in the armed forces.

prov.heading: Application of section 17(4) to veterans with qualifying operational service

para.text: Section 17(4) does not preclude the production of evidence (whether strictly legal evidence or not) showing that any conditions that resulted in the disablement or death of the person were possibly service-related.
If that evidence is produced, then (in the absence of evidence to the contrary), the person's condition is to be treated as service-related.

prov.heading: Presumption that injury, illness, or death due to qualifying operational service

para.text: For the purposes of this Act, a veteran who is injured, becomes ill, or dies is presumed to have been injured, become ill, or died as a result of performing qualifying operational service if the injury, illness, or death occurred while the veteran was performing qualifying operational service.
For the purposes of this Act, the aggravation of an injury or illness of a veteran is presumed to be the result of performing qualifying operational service if—
the injury or illness was sustained or suffered before the veteran began performing qualifying operational service; and

the injury or illness was recorded in the service medical records before the veteran commenced qualifying operational service, but only where the injury or illness was known to the veteran before commencing qualifying operational service; and
the aggravation to the injury or illness occurred during the performance of qualifying operational service.
The presumptions in subsections (1) and (2) are conclusive.

prov.heading: Presumption in relation to paired organs

para.text: This section applies if—
a veteran suffers the loss of, or the permanent loss of the use of, one of any paired organs of his or her body; and
that loss is service-related; and
the veteran subsequently loses or suffers impaired efficiency of the other corresponding organ (from whatever cause).
The subsequent loss or impaired efficiency of the corresponding organ is presumed to be service-related.
The presumption in subsection (2) is conclusive.
Regulations may be made under section 265 on the recommendation of the Minister specifying which organs are paired organs for the purposes of this section.
The Minister must not make a recommendation under subsection (4) without first consulting—
relevant medical organisations; and
the advisory panel; and
any other person or organisation that the Minister considers appropriate to consult.

prov.heading: Conclusively presumed injuries, illnesses, and conditions

para.text: Regulations made under section 265 on the recommendation of the Minister may specify injuries, illnesses, and conditions that must be treated as service-related if a veteran has—
been exposed to specific events during qualifying operational service; or
served at specified times in, or during specified periods of, or in specified deployments during, qualifying operational service.
The Minister must not make a recommendation under subsection (1) without first consulting—
relevant medical organisations; and
the advisory panel; and
any other person or organisation that the Minister considers appropriate to consult.

prov.heading: Statements of principles made under Australian legislation

para.text: As soon as practicable after the commencement of this section, VANZ must undertake and complete a review of the statements of principles determined by the Australian Repatriation Medical Authority under the Veterans' Entitlements Act 1986 (Aust).
The purpose of the review is to assess which of the statements of principles should be applied in New Zealand.
In conducting the review, VANZ must consult, and have regard to the views of,—
the Royal New Zealand Returned and Services' Association Incorporated; and
relevant medical organisations; and
any other person or organisation that VANZ considers appropriate to consult.
After completing the review, VANZ must prepare and provide a report to the Minister and the advisory panel.
As soon as practicable after receiving the report and any comments on it from the advisory panel, the Minister must decide which statements of principles should apply in New Zealand.
Regulations may be made under section 265 on the recommendation of the Minister specifying that a statement of principles applies for the purposes of this Act.
Any assessment of a statement of principles or consultation on a statement of principles includes any assessment or consultation begun or completed before the commencement of this section.

prov.heading: New statements of principles and revocation of statements of principles

para.text: As soon as practicable after VANZ becomes aware of a new statement of principles being issued or of a statement of principles being revoked, VANZ must—
review the new statement of principles or the revocation of the statement of principles; and
prepare and provide a report to the Minister and the advisory panel.
As soon as practicable after receiving the report and any comments on it from the advisory panel, the Minister must decide,—
in the case of a new statement of principles, whether the statement of principles should apply in New Zealand; and
in the case of a revocation of a statement of principles, whether the statement of principles should cease to apply in New Zealand.
Regulations may be made under section 265 on the recommendation of the Minister,—
in the case of a new statement of principles, specifying that the statement of principles applies for the purposes of this Act; and
in the case of a revocation of a statement of principles, specifying that the statement of principles ceases to apply for the purposes of this Act.

prov.heading: Amendments to statements of principles

para.text: As soon as practicable after VANZ becomes aware of a statement of principles that applies in New Zealand being amended, VANZ must—
review the amendment; and
prepare and provide a report to—
the Minister; and
if the amendment is not a minor amendment, the advisory panel.

As soon as practicable after receiving the report and any comments on it by the advisory panel (in the case of an amendment that is not minor), the Minister must decide whether the amendment should apply in New Zealand.
The Minister may, by notice, specify that the amendment applies for the purposes of this Act.
A notice under this section is secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: The maker must publish it in the Gazette
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 24(3): amended, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).
Section 24(4): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: Application of certain sections of Legislation Act 2019

para.text: Section 66 and clauses 2 to 7 of Schedule 2 of the Legislation Act 2019 apply to the following as if they incorporated material by reference and were made in reliance on section 64 of that Act:
regulations made under section 265 for the purposes of section 22 or 23; and
a notice under section 24.

history.history-note: Section 25: replaced, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: Proof of statements of principles

para.text: The production in proceedings of a statement of principles or an amendment of a statement of principles, certified by the Chief of Defence Force as applying in New Zealand, is, in the absence of proof to the contrary, sufficient evidence that the statement of principles or the amendment of the statement of principles applies in New Zealand.

prov.heading: Responsibility of veteran or other claimant to assist in establishing and maintaining entitlements

para.text: A veteran or other claimant who applies for or receives an entitlement under this Act must, when reasonably required to do so by VANZ,—
give VANZ a certificate by a health practitioner or treatment provider that deals with the matters or contains the information that VANZ requires:
authorise VANZ to obtain medical and other records that are or may be relevant to the application or entitlement:
undergo assessment at VANZ's expense by an assessor appointed by VANZ under section 212 and specified by VANZ.
A veteran or other claimant who applies for or receives an entitlement under this Act must, when reasonably required by VANZ,—
give VANZ relevant information about any matters relating to his or her application or entitlement that VANZ requires at any time:
undergo any assessment in relation to the veteran's or claimant's application or entitlement that VANZ may require under this Act:
participate in rehabilitation, including—
co-operating with VANZ in the development and implementation of any individual rehabilitation plan; and
undergoing, at VANZ's expense, assessment of present and likely capabilities for the purposes of rehabilitation.
If VANZ requires the veteran or other claimant to do so, he or she must provide the information referred to in subsection (2)﻿(a) in a form supplied or specified by VANZ.
If a veteran or other claimant fails to comply with a requirement under subsection (1) or (2), VANZ may cease payment of an entitlement to which the requirement relates.
VANZ may cease payment of an entitlement under subsection (4) only if—
VANZ has given the veteran or other claimant written notice of its intention to cease payment because the veteran or other claimant has failed to comply with the requirement; and
the veteran or other claimant has not, within 28 days of the date of giving the notice,—
complied with the requirement; or
established, to VANZ's satisfaction, reasonable grounds for not complying with the requirement.

history.history-note: Section 27(1): amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 10(1) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 27(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 10(2) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Circumstances that exclude or limit entitlements

para.text: Claims to entitlements under this Act are excluded if the injury, illness, or death concerned occurred in any of the following circumstances:
while the veteran was a deserter or absent without leave within the meaning of the relevant armed forces disciplinary legislation applicable at the time of the injury, illness, or death:
while the veteran was committing an offence under New Zealand law or committing an act overseas that, had it been done in New Zealand, would have been an offence under New Zealand law:
in the case of death, the death was self-inflicted (unless occurring during qualifying operational service), but only in relation to entitlements under Part 4 (Scheme Two):

the injury, illness, or death was predominantly caused by or significantly aggravated by the use of alcohol or tobacco products, the misuse of illegal or prescription drugs, or the contracting of a sexually transmitted disease, but only in relation to entitlements under Part 4 (Scheme Two).

Despite subsection (1), VANZ may provide entitlements that would otherwise be excluded by subsection (1)﻿(a) or (b) if VANZ is satisfied on reasonable grounds that, in the circumstances, the exclusion would be unfair to the veteran (for example, because the desertion or absence without leave was brief or the offence was minor in nature).
Despite subsection (1), VANZ may provide entitlements that would otherwise be excluded by subsection (1)﻿(c) or (d) if VANZ is satisfied on reasonable grounds that—
the injury, illness, or death was due to a psychological condition that was attributed to or aggravated by the experiences of the veteran during qualifying operational service; or

the injury or illness is itself a psychological condition (for example, alcohol use disorder) that was attributed to or aggravated by the experiences of the veteran during qualifying operational service; or
in the case of a sexually transmitted disease, the disease resulted from suffering a sexual assault.

For the purposes of subsection (1)﻿(a), the finding of a military tribunal (within the meaning of the Armed Forces Discipline Act 1971) that a veteran was a deserter or absent without leave is conclusive proof that the veteran was a deserter or absent without leave.
Claims to entitlements under this Act in relation to injury or illness are excluded to the extent that the injury or illness is due wholly or substantially to the ageing process.

history.history-note: Section 28(1A): inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 11(1) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 28(2): amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 11(2) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 28(2)﻿(aa): inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 11(3) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Limited entitlements during imprisonment of veteran

para.text: While a veteran is a prisoner in any prison, VANZ—
may continue to provide to the veteran entitlements under subparts 2 and 3 of Part 5 that were being provided to the veteran before the veteran became a prisoner; but
must not provide any other entitlements under Part 3, 4, or 5 to the veteran.
Entitlements of other claimants are not affected by the imprisonment of the veteran.
While a claimant other than the veteran is a prisoner in any prison, VANZ must not provide, or continue to provide, any entitlements under Part 3, 4, or 5 to the claimant.
In this section,—
prison means—
a prison within the meaning of section 3(1) of the Corrections Act 2004; or
a service prison within the meaning of section 2(1) of the Armed Forces Discipline Act 1971; or
an overseas prison
prisoner means,—
for a prison referred to in paragraph (a) of the definition of prison, a prisoner within the meaning of section 3(1) of the Corrections Act 2004; or
for a prison referred to in paragraph (b) of that definition, a service prisoner within the meaning of section 2(1) of the Armed Forces Discipline Act 1971; or
for a prison referred to in paragraph (c) of that definition, a person for the time being in legal custody in that prison.

history.history-note: Section 29: replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 12 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Indexation of certain entitlements in relation to average weekly earnings

para.text: VANZ must adjust the following in the manner specified in subsection (2):
the rate of weekly compensation as calculated under Part 2 of Schedule 2:
the amount of weekly earnings under clause 44(1) of Schedule 2.
Any adjustments required by subsection (1)—
must be made in accordance with a prescribed formula or prescribed formulas relating to movements in average weekly earnings; and
have effect from the prescribed date or dates; and

must be published by VANZ on an Internet site maintained by or on behalf of VANZ and published in any other manner that VANZ thinks appropriate to bring the adjustments to the attention of claimants and the general public.
VANZ is not required to adjust any amount under subsection (1) if—
the prescribed formula specifies a minimum movement in average weekly earnings; and
the movement in average weekly earnings is less than the minimum movement specified.

prov.heading: Indexation of certain entitlements in relation to Consumers Price Index

para.text: VANZ must adjust the following in the manner specified in subsection (3):
the rates of disablement pension (section 56):
the rate of the surviving spouse or partner pension (section 66):
the rate of the children’s pension (section 72):
the rate of the dependant’s pension (section 76):
the rate of the children’s bursary (section 81):
the lump sum payment for permanent impairment (section 98):
the maximum amount for motor vehicle grants (section 148):
the amount of the retirement lump sum payment (section 151):
the asset threshold for non-exempt assets (section 151):
the maximum amount payable for funeral expenses for—
a veteran who had undertaken qualifying operational service; or
a veteran whose death was a service-related death (section 153):
the maximum amount payable under Scheme One for funeral expenses for other veterans (section 154):
the lump sum payment to a surviving spouse or partner of a veteran receiving the veteran’s pension (section 174):
the rates of annuities or allowances payable under section 29A of the War Pensions Act 1954 and Part 5A of the War Pensions Regulations 1956 (see clause 2 of Schedule 1 of this Act):
the survivor’s grant (clause 53 of Schedule 2):
child care payments for children of deceased veterans (clause 64 of Schedule 2).
VANZ may adjust in the manner specified in subsection (3) any costs, amounts, or limits on costs that are not required to be adjusted under section 30 or subsection (1) of this section.
Any adjustments under subsection (1) or (2)—
must be made in accordance with a prescribed formula or prescribed formulas relating to any movement in the Consumers Price Index or any group or subgroup within the Index; and
have effect from the prescribed date or dates; and

must be published by VANZ on an Internet site maintained by or on behalf of VANZ and published in any other manner that VANZ thinks appropriate to bring the adjustments to the attention of claimants and the general public.
VANZ is not required to adjust any amount under subsection (1) if—
the prescribed formula specifies a minimum movement in the Consumers Price Index or any group or subgroup within the Index; and
the movement in the Consumers Price Index, group, or subgroup is less than the minimum movement specified.

In this section, Consumers Price Index (all groups) means the Consumers Price Index (all groups) published by Statistics New Zealand or, if that index ceases to be published, any measure certified by the Government Statistician as being equivalent to that index.

history.history-note: Section 31 heading: amended, on 1 September 2022, by section 107(1) of the Data and Statistics Act 2022 (2022 No 39).
Section 31(1)﻿(j): replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 13 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 31(1)﻿(k): replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 13 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 31(1)﻿(la): inserted, on 24 October 2019, by section 174 of the Statutes Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 56).
Section 31(3)﻿(a): amended, on 1 September 2022, by section 107(1) of the Data and Statistics Act 2022 (2022 No 39).
Section 31(4)﻿(a): amended, on 1 September 2022, by section 107(1) of the Data and Statistics Act 2022 (2022 No 39).
Section 31(4)﻿(b): amended, on 1 September 2022, by section 107(1) of the Data and Statistics Act 2022 (2022 No 39).
Section 31(5): inserted, on 1 September 2022, by section 107(1) of the Data and Statistics Act 2022 (2022 No 39).

part.heading: Code of Veterans' and Other Claimants' Rights

prov.heading: Purpose of Code

para.text: The purpose of the Code is to meet the reasonable expectations of veterans and claimants (including the highest practicable standard of service and fairness) about how VANZ should deal with them, by—
specifying what VANZ will do, in performing its functions and exercising its powers, to ensure that it acts in accordance with the principles in section 10(b); and
conferring rights on veterans and claimants and imposing obligations on VANZ in relation to how VANZ should deal with the veterans and claimants; and
providing for the procedure for lodging and dealing with complaints about breaches of the Code by VANZ; and
providing—
for the consequences of, and remedies for, a breach of the Code by VANZ; and

without limiting subparagraph (i), how and to what extent VANZ must address situations where its conduct is not consistent with or does not uphold the rights of veterans and claimants under the Code; and
providing for and explaining a veteran's and claimant's right to a review of a decision made under the Code about a claimant's complaint.
The rights and obligations in the Code—
are in addition to any other rights veterans and claimants have, and obligations VANZ has, under this Act, any other enactment, or the general law; and
do not affect the entitlements and responsibilities of veterans and claimants under this Act, any other enactment, or the general law.

prov.heading: Chief of Defence Force to prepare draft Code in consultation with persons nominated by Minister

para.text: The Chief of Defence Force must, as soon as practicable after this section comes into force, prepare a draft Code of Veterans' and Other Claimants' Rights.

In preparing a draft Code, the Chief of Defence Force must consult such persons, bodies, organisations, and agencies as the Minister considers necessary to ensure that a wide range of views is available to the Chief of Defence Force to assist him or her in preparing a draft Code.
In subsection (2), organisation includes the Royal New Zealand Returned and Services' Association Incorporated and other groups representing veterans and claimants.
For the purposes of subsection (2), the Minister may take into account any relevant consultation undertaken by the Chief of Defence Force before this section comes into force.
After preparing the draft Code, the Chief of Defence Force must forward it to the Minister.

prov.heading: Approval of draft Code by Minister

para.text: If the Minister agrees with the draft Code forwarded to him or her under section 33(5), the Minister must,—
approve the Code; and
present the Code to the House of Representatives within 12 sitting days after approving the Code; and
arrange for the approval of the Code to be notified in daily newspapers published in Auckland, Hamilton, Wellington, Christchurch, and Dunedin.
A notice given under subsection (1)﻿(c) must state where copies of the Code may be obtained.
The Code is secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).
That Act applies as if—
the Minister were the maker of the Code; and
the Code were made by the Minister approving it.

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation referred to in subsection (3)

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation referred to in subsection (3)

row.entry: • notify it in the Gazette with a statement of where copies of it can be obtained
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 34(1)﻿(a): amended, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).
Section 34(3): replaced, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).
Section 34(4): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: VANZ's obligations in respect of Code

para.text: VANZ must, in all its dealings with veterans and claimants, ensure that its actions are consistent with and uphold the rights of veterans and claimants in the Code.
VANZ must—
make the Code accessible to veterans and claimants and members of the public generally; and
promote awareness of the Code among veterans and claimants and members of the public generally.

prov.heading: Status of Code as disallowable instrument

history.history-note: Section 36: repealed, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: Amendments to Code

para.text: The Code may be amended by the Minister approving 1 or more amendments to the Code proposed by the Minister or the Chief of Defence Force.
Sections 33 and 34(1) and (2) apply, with all necessary modifications, in relation to an amendment to the Code as if the amendment were the preparation of a draft Code.
However, those provisions do not apply to an amendment if—
the amendment is a minor or technical amendment; and
the Minister considers that compliance with those provisions is unnecessary.
An amendment to the Code is secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).
That Act applies as if—
the Minister were the maker of the amendment; and
the amendment were made by the Minister approving it.

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation referred to in subsection (4).

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation referred to in subsection (4)

row.entry: • notify it in the Gazette with a statement of where copies of it can be obtained
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 37(1): amended, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).
Section 37(2): amended, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).
Section 37(4): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).
Section 37(5): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: Application of Part 3

para.text: This Part applies to the following persons:
every veteran with qualifying service performed—
before 1 April 1974; or
before 31 March 1975, in connection with the civilian surgical team based at Qui Nhon Provincial State Hospital, Binh Dinh province, Vietnam; or
before 21 April 1975, in connection with 41 Squadron RNZAF in Vietnam:
every spouse, partner, child, and dependant of every veteran referred to in paragraph (a).
This Part also applies in relation to a veteran's claim if VANZ has determined under section 84(2) that the claim is to be dealt with under this Part.

prov.heading: Application of Part 3 before commencement of Part 4 (Scheme Two)

para.text: This Part also applies to claims made before the commencement of Part 4 (Scheme Two) by the following persons:
every veteran with qualifying operational service performed in the period beginning on 1 April 1974 and ending on the day before the date on which Part 4 (Scheme Two) comes into force; and
every spouse, partner, child, and dependant of every veteran referred to in paragraph (a).

prov.heading: Purpose of Scheme One

para.text: The purpose of Scheme One is to provide entitlements to veterans and their spouses, partners, children, and dependants who are eligible under section 38 or 39.
The entitlements are—
travel costs associated with treatment and long-term hospital care (subpart 2); and
the disablement pension (subpart 3); and
weekly income compensation (subpart 4); and
entitlements for spouses, partners, children, and dependants of deceased veterans (subpart 5); and
children's bursaries (subpart 6).

prov.heading: Other entitlements

para.text: Part 5 provides other entitlements that are common to Scheme One and Scheme Two.
Part 1 of Schedule 2 provides additional entitlements in relation to rehabilitation.

subpart.heading: Travel costs associated with treatment and long-term hospital care

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this subpart, unless the context otherwise requires,—
travel costs means the cost of—
travel:
meals and accommodation reasonably associated with the travel:
actual and reasonable out-of-pocket expenses associated with the travel.

prov.heading: When travel costs payable

para.text: VANZ is responsible for paying or contributing to the travel costs of a veteran in the following situations:
where the veteran has to undergo treatment for a service-related injury or illness:
where VANZ requires the veteran to undergo a medical assessment for the purpose of determining whether or to what extent a veteran can claim an entitlement under this Part:
where the veteran is required to undergo a medical assessment for the purposes of a review of or appeal against a decision relating to the veteran's entitlement under this Part.

prov.heading: Travel escort

para.text: This section applies where VANZ is responsible for paying or contributing to the cost of travel under section 43.

VANZ is also responsible for paying or contributing towards paying the cost of a travel escort for a veteran if a health practitioner certifies, in writing, that the veteran is not fit or able to travel alone.

prov.heading: Travel costs of certain persons visiting veteran in long-term hospital care

para.text: VANZ is responsible for paying or contributing to the reasonable travel costs of the persons specified in subsection (2) who are visiting a veteran who is in long-term hospital care.
The persons are—
the veteran's spouse or partner:
any children or dependants of the veteran:
any other person if VANZ considers it appropriate to pay or contribute to pay the cost of the person to visit the veteran.

prov.heading: Regulations

para.text: Regulations may be made under section 265 specifying—
the maximum amounts payable for travel costs under this subpart:
the process for claiming and paying travel costs under this subpart:
any other matters for the effective and efficient administration of this subpart.

prov.heading: Disablement pension

para.text: A veteran (whether resident in New Zealand or overseas) who suffers disablement as a consequence of a service-related injury or illness is entitled to a disablement pension.
However, a veteran is not entitled to a temporary disablement pension unless he or she—
is participating in a rehabilitation plan; or
has a reasonable excuse for not participating in a rehabilitation plan in relation to the veteran's service-related injury or illness.

prov.heading: Application for disablement pension

para.text: A veteran becomes entitled to a disablement pension when—
the veteran has completed and provided to VANZ an application form provided by VANZ; and
the veteran has supplied, in the form required by VANZ, any supporting evidence or information required by VANZ; and
VANZ has accepted the application.
If an application is accepted by VANZ, the veteran's entitlement to the disablement pension is to be treated as beginning on the day on which VANZ received the application.

prov.heading: Disablement pension temporary unless specified to be permanent

para.text: A disablement pension is temporary unless VANZ, when notifying a veteran that his or her application for a disablement pension has been accepted, notifies the veteran that his or her disablement pension is permanent.
A disablement pension may be notified as permanent if VANZ, on the basis of medical and other relevant evidence, considers that—
the veteran's condition is not likely to improve; or
the veteran's condition has stabilised or reached its final state.

If a disablement pension is temporary, VANZ must advise a veteran that his or her disablement pension is temporary and must state the date by which the veteran's disablement is to be reassessed. VANZ may, if it considers it appropriate in the circumstances, advise the veteran of a later date by which the veteran's disablement is to be reassessed.
On or before the date specified under subsection (3), VANZ must reassess the veteran's disablement to determine whether—
the veteran's disablement pension should be cancelled because the veteran is no longer suffering from a medical condition that entitles him or her to receive a disablement pension; or
the veteran's temporary disablement pension should continue because the medical condition that entitles the veteran to receive a disablement pension has not stabilised or reached its final state; or
the veteran's temporary disablement pension should be made permanent because the condition causing the veteran's disablement has stabilised or reached its final state.

To avoid doubt, a veteran's temporary disablement pension may be made permanent, even though the veteran is assessed at 0% whole-person impairment and therefore not entitled to a disablement pension, on either or both of the following grounds:
the need for ongoing treatment to deal with the effects of the disablement:
the potential for the disablement to return or recur.
If VANZ makes a determination under subsection (4)﻿(b), it must notify the veteran of a further date by which the veteran's disablement is to be reassessed.

For the purposes of the reassessment under subsection (6), subsection (4) applies with all necessary modifications as if the reference to the date in subsection (3) were a reference to the date specified under subsection (6).

prov.heading: Suspension of temporary disablement pension

para.text: VANZ may suspend payment of a temporary disablement pension to a veteran if VANZ is unable to complete a reassessment of the veteran's disablement under section 52 because the veteran has, without reasonable excuse, not complied with any reasonable requirement by VANZ for the purpose of completing the reassessment.

However, VANZ must not suspend payment of a temporary disablement pension under subsection (1) unless it has given the veteran not less than 28 days' notice in writing that it proposes to suspend payment of the pension.

prov.heading: Treatment and rehabilitation during period of temporary disablement pension

para.text: VANZ must, in consultation with a veteran whose disablement pension is temporary, prepare a treatment and rehabilitation plan for the veteran to follow.

prov.heading: Reassessment of veteran's disablement

para.text: VANZ may, from time to time, reassess the disablement of a veteran who is receiving a permanent disablement pension.
A reassessment under subsection (1) must not be undertaken earlier than 2 years—
after the veteran's disablement pension became permanent; or
after the veteran's last whole-person impairment assessment.
However, a reassessment may be undertaken earlier if—
the veteran provides medical evidence to the satisfaction of VANZ that the veteran's disablement has increased significantly; or
VANZ considers that the veteran's disablement has changed significantly.
For the purposes of subsection (3), a change in disablement is significant if the change in whole-person impairment is 10% or more.

prov.heading: Lump sum payment for terminal conditions

para.text: A veteran who is entitled to a disablement pension may elect to—
suspend payment of the pension for a period of 12 months; and
receive a lump sum payment equal to the aggregate of the pension payable at the maximum rate for the 12-month period.
A veteran may make an election under subsection (1)—
only once if the election is accepted by VANZ; and
only if the veteran produces evidence to the satisfaction of VANZ that the veteran has a terminal medical condition that is a service-related condition.

For the purposes of subsection (1), if VANZ accepts that the veteran may make an election under this section, the 12-month period is to be treated as starting on the day on which VANZ receives the veteran's election.
Schedule 4 contains a consequential amendment to the Income Tax Act 2007 that exempts a lump sum payment under this section from income tax.
If, during the 12-month period, the rate of payment of the disablement pension is adjusted, the veteran is entitled to an additional lump sum payment equal to the difference between—
what the veteran received under subsection (1); and
what the veteran would have received under subsection (1) had the adjustment to the rate and the date of the adjustment been taken into account when the lump sum was paid.

If a veteran who receives a lump sum payment under this section is still alive at the close of the 12-month period referred to in subsection (1)﻿(b), the veteran is entitled to the maximum disablement pension on and from the day after the close of that period.
In subsection (2)﻿(b) and section 54, terminal medical condition means an advanced progressive disease likely to cause death within the 12-month period referred to in subsection (1).

prov.heading: Maximum disablement pension payable if veteran has terminal condition

para.text: A veteran may apply to VANZ to increase his or her disablement pension to the maximum rate.
VANZ may accept the application if the veteran produces evidence to the satisfaction of VANZ that the veteran has a terminal medical condition that is a service-related medical condition.
This section does not prevent a veteran from subsequently making an election under section 53.

prov.heading: Entitlement to disablement pension ceases 28 days after veteran's death

para.text: When a veteran who is receiving a disablement pension dies, the entitlement to the pension ceases at the close of the 28th day after the date on which the veteran dies.
This section does not apply in relation to a veteran who has elected to receive a lump sum payment under section 53 and dies during the 12-month period referred to in that section.

prov.heading: Rate of disablement pension

para.text: The rates of the disablement pension are to be set by regulations made under section 265.
The regulations must set different rates for different levels of whole-person impairment.

prov.heading: Increased pension if treatment requires veteran's absence from home or work

para.text: VANZ may increase the rate of disablement pension payable to a veteran if—
the veteran is undergoing any treatment; and
VANZ is satisfied that the treatment requires the veteran to live away from home or be absent from work for 1 or more days in any week.
An increase in the rate of disablement pension under subsection (1)—
applies only for the period of the treatment; and
must not result in the rate of the pension exceeding the rate that would be payable if the veteran were totally disabled.

prov.heading: Transitional provisions

para.text: Schedule 1 contains transitional provisions relating to veterans who are receiving a war disablement pension under Part 2 of the War Pensions Act 1954 immediately before the commencement of this Part.

prov.heading: Weekly income compensation

para.text: A veteran (whether resident in New Zealand or overseas) is entitled to weekly income compensation in accordance with this subpart if the veteran is—
under the New Zealand superannuation qualification age; and
unable to work full-time; and
either—
participating in a rehabilitation plan; or
through no fault of the veteran, unable to participate in a rehabilitation plan in relation to the veteran's service-related injury or illness; and
not receiving any benefit under the Social Security Act 2018.
If a veteran is resident overseas, the veteran is responsible for paying the costs of assessing whether the veteran is unable to work full-time.
In this subpart, unable to work means unable to work as a consequence of injury or illness from whatever cause.

history.history-note: Section 59(1)﻿(d): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: Application for weekly income compensation

para.text: A veteran becomes entitled to weekly income compensation when—
the veteran has completed and provided to VANZ an application form provided by VANZ; and
the veteran has supplied, in the form required by VANZ, any supporting evidence or information required by VANZ; and
VANZ has accepted the application.
If an application is accepted by VANZ, the veteran's entitlement to weekly income compensation is to be treated as beginning on the day on which VANZ received the application.

prov.heading: Rate of weekly income compensation

para.text: Weekly income compensation is payable at the rate that is 80% of the average wage.
In this section, average wage means the before tax average ordinary time weekly wage (all sectors, male and female combined) as at 1 April each year, as specified in the latest QES.

history.history-note: Section 61(2): replaced, on 1 September 2022, by section 107(1) of the Data and Statistics Act 2022 (2022 No 39).

prov.heading: Entitlement to weekly income compensation ceases if veteran able to work full-time

para.text: This section applies to a veteran who is under the New Zealand superannuation qualification age and is receiving weekly income compensation.

If the veteran has been receiving weekly income compensation for less than 6 months and VANZ assesses the veteran as able to work full-time in employment in which he or she was employed immediately before receiving weekly income compensation, the veteran ceases to be entitled to weekly income compensation on the expiry of 10 days after receiving notice of the assessment.

If the veteran has been receiving weekly income compensation for 6 months or more and VANZ assesses the veteran as able to work full-time in employment in which he or she was employed immediately before receiving weekly income compensation, the veteran ceases to be entitled to weekly income compensation on the earlier of—
the date on which the veteran commences full-time employment; or
the expiry of 28 days after the assessment.

If the veteran is not able to work full-time in employment in which he or she was employed immediately before receiving weekly income compensation, but VANZ assesses the veteran as able to undertake other full-time employment, then the veteran ceases to be entitled to weekly income compensation on the earlier of—
the date on which the veteran commences full-time employment; or
the expiry of 28 days after the assessment.

prov.heading: Entitlement to compensation reduces if veteran able to work part-time

para.text: This section applies to a veteran who is under the New Zealand superannuation qualification age and is receiving weekly income compensation.

If the veteran undertakes part-time work, VANZ must reduce the amount of weekly income compensation payable to the veteran to ensure that the total of the veteran’s weekly income compensation and earnings does not exceed the average wage.
In subsection (2), average wage has the same meaning as in section 61(2).

prov.heading: Extent to which veteran entitled to weekly income compensation under this subpart and under Accident Compensation Act 2001

para.text: A veteran who has cover under the Accident Compensation Act 2001 is not entitled to weekly income compensation under this subpart and under the Accident Compensation Act 2001.
However, the veteran is entitled to receive from VANZ the difference between the weekly income compensation payable under this subpart and under the Accident Compensation Act 2001.

prov.heading: Relationship between weekly income compensation and New Zealand superannuation or veteran’s pension

para.text: Subsection (2) applies to a veteran who—
first becomes entitled to weekly income compensation before reaching New Zealand superannuation qualification age; and
has been entitled to it for 24 months or longer before reaching that age.
The veteran loses entitlement to weekly income compensation on reaching that age.
Subsection (4) applies to a veteran who first becomes entitled to weekly income compensation—
less than 24 months before reaching New Zealand superannuation qualification age; or
on or after reaching New Zealand superannuation qualification age.
The veteran is entitled to weekly income compensation for 24 months from the date of entitlement to the compensation.
Nothing in this section entitles a veteran to weekly income compensation if the veteran is not otherwise entitled to it under this Act.

history.history-note: Section 65: replaced, on 1 July 2019, by section 13 of the Accident Compensation Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 10).

subpart.heading: Spouses, partners, children, and dependants of veterans

prov.heading: Surviving spouse or partner pension

para.text: A spouse or partner of a deceased veteran is entitled, in accordance with this section, to a surviving spouse or partner pension at a rate specified in regulations made under section 265.
This section applies if—
the veteran had undertaken qualifying operational service; or
the veteran’s death was a service-related death; or
at the time of the veteran’s death, the veteran was receiving—
a permanent war disablement pension under Part 2 of the War Pensions Act 1954 in relation to disablement of 70% or more; or
a permanent disablement pension granted under subpart 3 in relation to whole-person impairment of 52% or more; or

VANZ considers that, had the veteran not died, the veteran would have been eligible to receive a pension specified in paragraph (c) (to the extent of impairment specified) had he or she applied for it immediately before his or her death.
This section does not apply if, immediately before the veteran’s death,—
the veteran and the claimant were living apart or were not maintaining a relationship in the nature of marriage; and
the claimant was not contributing to the veteran’s day-to-day welfare.

Despite subsection (3), this section applies if the circumstances described in that subsection occurred principally because of the health, imprisonment, or employment obligations of the veteran or the claimant.

history.history-note: Section 66: replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 14 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Application for surviving spouse or partner pension

para.text: A surviving spouse or partner becomes entitled to a surviving spouse or partner pension when—
the surviving spouse or partner has completed and provided to VANZ an application form provided by VANZ; and
the surviving spouse or partner has supplied, in the form required by VANZ, any supporting evidence or information required by VANZ; and
VANZ has accepted the application.
If an application is accepted by VANZ, the surviving spouse's or partner's entitlement to a surviving spouse or partner pension is to be treated as beginning on—
the day after the veteran's death, if the application is received by VANZ within 6 months after the veteran's death; or
the day on which VANZ received the application, if the application is received by VANZ more than 6 months after the veteran's death.

prov.heading: Surviving spouse or partner pension ceases after spouse or partner enters into new relationship

para.text: If the spouse or partner of a deceased veteran enters into a new relationship, the spouse's or partner's entitlement to the surviving spouse or partner pension ceases immediately.
However, a spouse or partner who enters into a new relationship may elect to receive—
a periodic payment for 2 years at the same rate as the surviving spouse or partner pension; or
a lump sum equivalent to 2 years’ payments of the surviving spouse or partner pension.
After an election is made under subsection (2), the entitlement to the option elected is to be treated as having begun on the day after the date on which the pension ceases under subsection (1).
For the purposes of this section, a spouse or partner of a deceased veteran enters into a new relationship when, after the veteran's death, the spouse or partner—
marries; or
enters into a civil union; or
enters into a de facto relationship.

prov.heading: Reinstatement of surviving spouse or partner pension

para.text: The spouse or partner of a deceased veteran whose entitlement to the surviving spouse or partner pension has ceased under section 68 is entitled to a reinstatement of his or her entitlement to the pension if the relationship he or she entered into that caused the termination of the pension under section 68 comes to an end within 5 years after the start of the relationship.

prov.heading: Entitlement to surviving spouse or partner pension ceases 28 days after spouse's or partner's death

para.text: When a surviving spouse or partner who is receiving a surviving spouse or partner pension dies, the entitlement to the pension ceases at the close of the 28th day after the date on which the surviving spouse or partner dies.

prov.heading: Transitional provisions

para.text: Schedule 1 contains transitional provisions relating to spouses and partners of veterans who were receiving a surviving spouse pension under Part 2 of the War Pensions Act 1954 immediately before the commencement of this Part.

prov.heading: Children's pension

para.text: Every child who is under 18 years of age and described in subsection (2) is entitled to a children's pension at the rate specified in regulations made under section 265.
The children are—
a child of a veteran who,—
immediately before the commencement of this section, was receiving a war disablement pension under Part 2 of the War Pensions Act 1954 in relation to disablement of 70% or more; or
is receiving a disablement pension granted under subpart 3 in relation to whole-person impairment of 52% or more; or
a child of a deceased veteran, if—
the veteran's death was a service-related death; or
at the time of the veteran's death, the veteran was receiving—
a permanent war disablement pension under Part 2 of the War Pensions Act 1954 in relation to disablement of 70% or more; or
a permanent disablement pension granted under subpart 3 in relation to whole-person impairment of 52% or more; or

VANZ considers that, had the veteran not died, the veteran would have been eligible to receive a pension or other payment specified in paragraph (b)﻿(ii)﻿(A) or (B) (to the extent of impairment specified) had he or she applied for it immediately before his or her death.

If the child suffers from any mental or physical infirmity, VANZ may, in its discretion, extend the entitlement beyond the date on which the child turns 18 years of age for as long as the child continues to suffer from the mental or physical infirmity.
If the child is undertaking full-time study, he or she is entitled to continue to receive the children's pension until the date on which he or she turns 23.

prov.heading: Application for children's pension

para.text: A child becomes entitled to a children's pension when—
the child has completed and provided to VANZ an application form provided by VANZ; and
the child has supplied, in the form required by VANZ, any supporting evidence or information required by VANZ; and
VANZ has accepted the application.
If an application is accepted by VANZ, the child's entitlement to the children's pension is to be treated as beginning on—
the day on which VANZ received the application, if the veteran is living; or
the day after the veteran's death, if the application is received by VANZ within 6 months after the veteran's death; or
the day on which VANZ received the application, if the application is received by VANZ more than 6 months after the veteran's death.

prov.heading: Entitlement to children's pension ceases 28 days after child's death

para.text: When a child who is receiving a children's pension dies, the entitlement to the pension ceases at the close of the 28th day after the date on which the child died.

prov.heading: Transitional provisions

para.text: Schedule 1 contains transitional provisions relating to children of veterans who were receiving a children's pension under Part 2 of the War Pensions Act 1954 immediately before the commencement of this Part.

prov.heading: Dependant's pension

para.text: A dependant described in subsection (2) is entitled to a dependant's pension at the rate specified in regulations made under section 265.
The dependants are—
a dependant of a veteran who,—
immediately before the commencement of this section, was receiving a war disablement pension under Part 2 of the War Pensions Act 1954 in relation to disablement of 70% or more; or
is receiving a disablement pension granted under subpart 3 in relation to whole-person impairment of 52% or more; or
a dependant of a deceased veteran, if—
the veteran's death was a service-related death; or
at the time of the veteran's death, the veteran was receiving—
a permanent war disablement pension under Part 2 of the War Pensions Act 1954 in relation to disablement of 70% or more; or
