a permanent disablement pension granted under subpart 3 in relation to whole-person impairment of 52% or more; or

VANZ considers that, had the veteran not died, the veteran would have been eligible to receive a pension or other payment specified in paragraph (b)﻿(ii)﻿(A) or (B) (to the extent of impairment specified) had he or she applied for it immediately before his or her death.
Regulations made under subsection (1) must—
provide for VANZ to be able to take into account a dependant's economic position; and
specify a maximum rate that does not exceed the maximum rate payable for the surviving spouse or partner pension.
For the purposes of subsection (3)﻿(a), the regulations—
must specify the criteria that VANZ is to take into account in assessing a dependant's economic position, including (but not limited to) the property and income of a dependant from all sources; and
may specify different rates for different types of economic circumstances.

prov.heading: Application for dependant's pension

para.text: A dependant becomes entitled to a dependant's pension when—
the dependant has completed and provided to VANZ an application form provided by VANZ; and
the dependant has supplied, in the form required by VANZ, any supporting evidence or information required by VANZ; and
VANZ has accepted the application.
If an application is accepted by VANZ, the dependant's entitlement to the dependant's pension is to be treated as beginning on—
the day on which VANZ received the application, if the veteran is living; or
the day after the veteran's death, if the application is received by VANZ within 6 months after the veteran's death; or
the day on which VANZ received the application, if the application is received by VANZ more than 6 months after the veteran's death.

prov.heading: Entitlement to dependant's pension ceases 28 days after dependant's death

para.text: When a dependant who is receiving a dependant's pension dies, the entitlement to the pension ceases at the close of the 28th day after the date on which the dependant dies.

prov.heading: Certain children not entitled to dependant's pension

para.text: A child who is receiving a children's pension under section 72(3) or (4) is not entitled to a dependant's pension.

prov.heading: Transitional provisions

para.text: Schedule 1 contains transitional provisions relating to dependants of veterans who were receiving a dependant's pension under Part 2 of the War Pensions Act 1954 immediately before the commencement of this Part.

prov.heading: Entitlement to children's bursary

para.text: Every child described in subsection (2) is entitled to a children's bursary if they are undertaking—
full-time study at a secondary school; or
full-time or part-time study at a tertiary education organisation; or
if the child resides overseas, study overseas that is equivalent to the study described in paragraph (a) or (b).
The children are—
children who are entitled to a children's pension:
children of veterans who are receiving weekly income compensation under subpart 4 of this Part or the veteran's pension under Part 6.
The entitlement to a children's bursary ceases at the end of the year in which a child reaches 23 years of age.
The rate of the children's bursary is to be prescribed in regulations made under section 265, and more than 1 rate may be specified for different circumstances.
In this section, secondary school and tertiary education organisation have the same meanings as in section 10(1) of the Education and Training Act 2020.

history.history-note: Section 81(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).
Section 81(1)﻿(c): inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 15 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 81(5): replaced, on 1 August 2020, by section 668 of the Education and Training Act 2020 (2020 No 38).

prov.heading: Transitional provisions

para.text: Schedule 1 contains transitional provisions relating to children who, before the commencement of this Part, were entitled to a war bursary under the War Pensions Act 1954.

prov.heading: Application

para.text: This Part—
applies to the following persons:
every veteran with qualifying operational service performed on or after 1 April 1974; and
every spouse, partner, child, and dependant of every veteran referred to in subparagraph (i); but
does not apply to the following persons:
the veterans referred to in section 38(1)﻿(a)﻿(ii) and (iii); and
every spouse, partner, child, and dependant of every veteran referred to in subparagraph (i).

prov.heading: Application in other circumstances

para.text: Subsection (2) applies to a claim that—
is made by or in relation to a veteran referred to in section 83(a)﻿(i); and
relates to injury, illness, or death that is attributable in part to circumstances before 1 April 1974 and in part to circumstances on or after 1 April 1974.
This Part applies to the claim unless VANZ determines that the claim should be dealt with under Part 3.
This Part does not apply to claims that Part 3 applies to under section 39.

prov.heading: Purpose of Scheme Two

para.text: The purpose of Scheme Two is to provide entitlements to veterans and other persons who are eligible under section 83 or 84.
The entitlements are—
transport costs associated with treatment (subpart 2); and
vocational services for spouses or partners (subpart 3); and
payments for permanent impairment (subpart 4); and
weekly compensation (subpart 5).

prov.heading: Other entitlements

para.text: Part 5 provides other entitlements that are common to Scheme One and Scheme Two.
Parts 2 to 4 of Schedule 2 provide additional entitlements in relation to rehabilitation, weekly compensation, and fatal injuries.

prov.heading: Extent to which entitlements provided by this Part

para.text: This section specifies the extent to which a veteran has an entitlement under this Part for injury or illness that is related to qualifying operational service.
If a veteran is a member of the Defence Force and has cover under the Accident Compensation Act 2001,—

the Defence Force is responsible for managing the veteran's claim and VANZ is to provide the entitlements in relation to the claim to the extent that the claim comes within an accreditation agreement (if any) between the Defence Force and the Accident Compensation Corporation (ACC):

ACC is responsible for managing the veteran's claim and providing the entitlements in relation to the claim to the extent that the claim is not subject to an accreditation agreement (if any) between the Defence Force and ACC.

If a veteran is a member of the Defence Force and does not have cover under the Accident Compensation Act 2001, the Defence Force is responsible for managing the veteran's claim and VANZ is to provide entitlements in relation to the claim in accordance with this Part.

If a veteran is not a member of the Defence Force and has cover under the Accident Compensation Act 2001, ACC is responsible for managing the claim and providing the entitlements in relation to the claim.

If a veteran is not a member of the Defence Force and does not have cover under the Accident Compensation Act 2001, VANZ is responsible for managing the veteran's claim and providing the entitlements in relation to the claim in accordance with this Part.
Subsections (1) to (5) apply subject to any agreement or arrangement between the Defence Force, VANZ, and ACC under which VANZ—
manages a veteran's claim and provides entitlements in relation to the claim; and
the claim is wholly or in part based on cover under the Accident Compensation Act 2001.

For the purposes of this section, the Defence Force, VANZ, and ACC must co-operate with each other to reduce, to the extent reasonably practicable, any duplication of process or requirements for a veteran referred to in subsection (6).

prov.heading: Transport costs associated with treatment

para.text: VANZ is responsible for paying the costs of transport reasonably required by the veteran in order to receive treatment that VANZ is responsible for paying or contributing to the cost of.
The extent to which VANZ is responsible for paying the costs of transport under subsection (1) is to be specified in regulations made under section 265.

subpart.heading: Vocational services and assistance for spouse or partner

prov.heading: Application of subpart

para.text: This subpart applies to the spouse or partner of a veteran if the veteran—
has an injury or illness—
that results from qualifying operational service; and
that makes the veteran unable to work or unable to undertake vocational rehabilitation; or
has died and his or her death is a service-related death.

prov.heading: Spouse or partner entitled to vocational services and assistance

para.text: The spouse or partner of a veteran to whom this subpart applies is entitled to be provided with vocational services and vocational assistance in accordance with this subpart and regulations made under section 265.

The purpose of providing vocational services and vocational assistance under subsection (1) is to reduce the financial impact on the veteran's spouse or partner and any dependants of the veteran of the veteran's inability to work or undertake vocational rehabilitation or of the veteran's death (as the case may be).

prov.heading: Period for which vocational assistance may be provided

para.text: VANZ must provide the vocational assistance to the spouse or partner for the minimum period necessary to achieve its purpose, but must not provide any vocational assistance for longer than 3 years (which need not be consecutive).
However, VANZ may, at its discretion, provide vocational assistance for longer than 3 years if VANZ considers that the vocational assistance would be appropriate in the circumstances.
VANZ must not take into account as a factor against providing vocational assistance that the spouse or partner is, or may be, a person to whom section 104 applies.

prov.heading: Assessment of spouse's or partner's vocational assistance needs

para.text: An assessment of a spouse's or partner's vocational assistance needs must consist of an initial occupational assessment, undertaken by an occupational assessor, to identify the types of work that may be appropriate for the spouse or partner.

prov.heading: Conduct of initial occupational assessment

para.text: An occupational assessor who is undertaking an initial occupational assessment must—
take into account information provided by VANZ and the spouse or partner; and
discuss with the spouse or partner all the types of work that are available and suitable for the spouse or partner; and
consider any comments the spouse or partner makes to the assessor about those types of work.
VANZ must provide to an occupational assessor all the information that VANZ has that is relevant to an initial occupational assessment.

prov.heading: Report on occupational assessment

para.text: The occupational assessor must prepare and provide to VANZ a report on the occupational assessment that specifies—
the types of work for the purposes of section 92, taking into account the information, discussions, and comments referred to in section 93; and
the spouse's or partner's work experience; and
the spouse's or partner's education, including any incomplete formal qualifications; and
any work-related training in which the spouse or partner has participated; and
all the skills that the assessor has reasonably identified the spouse or partner as having; and
all the types of work reasonably identified as suitable for the spouse or partner; and

in relation to each type of work, the requirements of that type of work, including any environmental modifications that the assessor identifies as necessary to enable the spouse or partner to function safely in that type of work.
VANZ must provide a copy of the report to the spouse or partner.

prov.heading: Entitlement to payment for permanent impairment

para.text: This subpart applies to a veteran if the veteran—
has an injury or illness that results from qualifying operational service; and
has a permanent impairment that is due to the injury or illness.

prov.heading: Independence allowance

para.text: An independence allowance is payable in relation to permanent impairment if the injury or illness that caused the permanent impairment occurred in the period beginning on 1 April 1974 and ending on the close of 31 March 2002.
An independence allowance is payable at the same rate and for the same levels of impairment as would be payable if a veteran were entitled to the disablement pension under subpart 3 of Part 3.
A veteran may, instead of receiving an independence allowance by way of periodic payments, elect to receive a lump sum payment in accordance with regulations made under section 265.
A lump sum payment must not be more than an amount that is equivalent to 5 years of the independence allowance.

prov.heading: Extent to which veteran entitled to independence allowance under this subpart and under Accident Compensation Act 2001

para.text: A veteran who has cover under the Accident Compensation Act 2001 is not entitled to an independence allowance under this subpart and an independence allowance under the Accident Compensation Act 2001 in relation to the same impairment.

However, the veteran is entitled to receive from VANZ the difference between the independence allowance payable under this subpart and the independence allowance payable under the Accident Compensation Act 2001.

prov.heading: Lump sum payments

para.text: A lump sum is payable in relation to permanent impairment if the injury or illness that caused the permanent impairment occurred on or after 1 April 2002.
A lump sum comprises—
the amount that—
is payable to the veteran under the Accident Compensation Act 2001, if the veteran is entitled to lump sum compensation for the impairment under that Act; or
if the veteran is not entitled to lump sum compensation for the impairment under that Act, would be payable if the veteran's impairment were covered under that Act; and
an additional amount equivalent to 20% of the amount payable under paragraph (a).

If the veteran's degree of impairment is below the level at which lump sums are payable under the Accident Compensation Act 2001, the amount for the purposes of subsection (2) is an amount specified in regulations made under section 265.
The amounts payable under subsections (2)﻿(a)﻿(ii) and (b) and (3) are payable by VANZ.

prov.heading: Impairment payments exempt from income tax

para.text: Schedule 4 contains a consequential amendment to the Income Tax Act 2007 that exempts payments of the independence allowance and of lump sums under this subpart from income tax.

prov.heading: Entitlement to weekly compensation

para.text: A veteran (whether resident in New Zealand or overseas) is entitled to weekly compensation if the veteran is—
under the New Zealand superannuation qualification age; and
unable to work because of injury or illness that is related to qualifying operational service; and
either—
participating in a rehabilitation plan; or
through no fault of the veteran, unable to participate in a rehabilitation plan.

prov.heading: Entitlement to compensation ceases or abates if veteran able to work

para.text: This section applies to a veteran who is under the New Zealand superannuation qualification age and is receiving weekly compensation.

If the veteran has been receiving weekly compensation for less than 6 months and VANZ assesses the veteran as able to work full-time in employment in which he or she was employed immediately before receiving weekly compensation, the veteran ceases to be entitled to weekly compensation on the expiry of 10 days after receiving notice of the assessment.

If the veteran has been receiving weekly compensation for 6 months or more and VANZ assesses the veteran as able to work full-time in employment in which he or she was employed immediately before receiving weekly compensation, the veteran ceases to be entitled to weekly compensation on the earlier of—
the date on which the veteran commences full-time employment; or
the expiry of 28 days after the assessment.

If the veteran is not able to work full-time in employment in which he or she was employed immediately before receiving weekly compensation, but VANZ assesses the veteran as able to undertake other full-time employment, then the veteran ceases to be entitled to weekly compensation on the earlier of—
the date on which the veteran commences full-time employment; or
the expiry of 28 days after the assessment.

prov.label: 101A
prov.heading: Entitlement to compensation ceases 28 days after veteran’s death

para.text: When a veteran who is receiving weekly compensation dies, the entitlement to the compensation ceases at the end of the 28th day after the date on which the veteran dies.

history.history-note: Section 101A: inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 16 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Extent to which veteran entitled to weekly compensation under this subpart and under Accident Compensation Act 2001

para.text: A veteran who has cover under the Accident Compensation Act 2001 is not entitled to weekly compensation under this subpart and under the Accident Compensation Act 2001.
However, the veteran is entitled to receive from VANZ the difference between the weekly compensation payable under this subpart and under the Accident Compensation Act 2001.

prov.heading: Weekly compensation is income for purposes of Income Tax Act 2007

para.text: Schedule 4 contains a consequential amendment to the Income Tax Act 2007 that makes payments of weekly compensation under this subpart income for the purposes of that Act.

prov.heading: Relationship between weekly compensation and New Zealand superannuation or veteran’s pension

para.text: Subsection (2) applies to a veteran who—
first becomes entitled to weekly compensation before reaching New Zealand superannuation qualification age; and
has been entitled to it for 24 months or longer before reaching that age.
The veteran loses entitlement to weekly compensation on reaching that age.
Subsection (4) applies to a veteran who first becomes entitled to weekly compensation—
less than 24 months before reaching New Zealand superannuation qualification age; or
on or after reaching New Zealand superannuation qualification age.
The veteran is entitled to weekly compensation for 24 months from the date of entitlement to the compensation.
Nothing in this section entitles a veteran to weekly compensation if the veteran is not otherwise entitled to it under this Act.

history.history-note: Section 104: replaced, on 1 July 2019, by section 14 of the Accident Compensation Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 10).

part.heading: Entitlements common to Scheme One and Scheme Two

prov.heading: Application

para.text: This Part applies to veterans to whom Part 3 or 4 applies.

prov.heading: Purpose of this Part

para.text: The purpose of this Part is to provide additional entitlements to, or in respect of, veterans to whom Scheme One or Scheme Two applies.
The entitlements are—
treatment (subpart 2); and
rehabilitation for veterans (subpart 3 and Part 1 of Schedule 2); and
participation in the veterans' independence programme (subpart 4); and
aids and appliances (subpart 5); and
motor vehicle grants (subpart 6); and
funeral expenses (subpart 8).

prov.heading: Treatment

para.text: VANZ is responsible for paying or contributing towards paying the cost of treatment of a veteran's injury or illness if the injury or illness is service-related.
In addition, VANZ is responsible for paying or contributing towards paying the cost of treatment in the form of counselling for—
the spouse or partner or any child or dependant of the veteran; or
a person (other than the veteran’s spouse, partner, child, or dependant) who provides non-professional support to the veteran.
VANZ must pay or contribute under subsection (1A) if VANZ is satisfied that—
the veteran has an injury or illness that is service-related; and
a need for counselling arises from that injury or illness.
However, VANZ is not responsible for paying or contributing to the cost of treatment if—
the veteran is a member of the armed forces and the Defence Force is paying or contributing to the cost of the treatment; or
the treatment is available under the Pae Ora (Healthy Futures) Act 2022; or
ACC is paying or contributing to the cost of the treatment.

history.history-note: Section 107(1A): inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 17(1) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 107(1B): inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 17(1) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 107(2)﻿(a): amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 17(2) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 107(2)﻿(b): amended, on 1 July 2022, by section 104 of the Pae Ora (Healthy Futures) Act 2022 (2022 No 30).
Section 107(2)﻿(c): amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 17(2) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Extent to which VANZ responsible for paying or contributing to cost of treatment

para.text: As soon as practicable after accepting an application for payment or contribution to payment of the costs of treatment under this subpart, VANZ must decide what treatment VANZ will pay for or contribute towards paying for.
In making a decision under subsection (1), VANZ must take into account the following matters:
the nature and severity of the injury or illness:
the generally accepted means of treating the injury or illness in New Zealand:
any other means of treating the injury or illness in New Zealand:
whether the treatment sought is necessary, appropriate, and of the quality required to treat the injury or illness:
whether the treatment will be provided only on the number of occasions necessary:
whether the treatment is of a type normally provided by the treatment provider for the injury or illness concerned:
whether the treatment provider is sufficiently qualified and experienced to provide the treatment required.

In addition, in making a decision about a claim for payment or a contribution under section 107(1A), VANZ must also take into account whether the claimant has a need for counselling arising from the veteran’s injury or illness.

history.history-note: Section 108(3): inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 18 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.label: 108A
prov.heading: Urgent treatment of mental injury or illness

para.text: This section applies if—
an application has been made for payment or contribution towards payment of the costs of treatment of a veteran’s injury or illness under this subpart; and
the treatment is or includes treatment for mental injury or illness; and
VANZ has not completed its consideration of the application; and
VANZ is satisfied that a delay in the provision of treatment for the mental injury or illness until the application is decided may have an adverse effect on the veteran.
Despite not having completed its consideration of the application, VANZ may pay or contribute towards paying the cost of treatment for the mental injury or illness.
Before deciding what treatment it will pay for or contribute towards paying for, VANZ must take into account the matters specified in section 108(2) (and, when applicable, section 108(3)).

history.history-note: Section 108A: inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 19 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: When VANZ responsible for paying or contributing to cost of ancillary services related to treatment

para.text: VANZ is responsible for paying or contributing to the cost of any service if the service facilitates the treatment and the service is reasonably required as an ancillary service related to treatment, such as—
accommodation:
pharmaceuticals prescribed by a treatment provider who has statutory authority to prescribe pharmaceuticals:
laboratory tests requested by a health practitioner.
This section applies subject to any regulations made under section 265.

prov.heading: Application for additional treatment

para.text: This section applies if VANZ is paying or contributing towards paying the cost of treatment under this subpart.
The person receiving the treatment may apply to VANZ—
to increase the amount VANZ is paying for or contributing towards paying the cost of treatment; or
to include additional treatment; or
to do both.
In deciding whether to grant an application under subsection (2), VANZ must take into account the matters specified in section 108(2).

history.history-note: Section 110(1): replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 20(1) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 110(2): amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 20(2) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Approval of VANZ required before treatment begins

para.text: VANZ is not responsible for paying or contributing to the cost of treatment under this subpart unless it has given its prior approval to the treatment being provided.
However, subsection (1) does not apply where VANZ is satisfied that treatment had to be provided in an emergency and it was not practicable to obtain VANZ's prior approval.

prov.heading: Appointment of case manager

para.text: As soon as practicable after accepting a veteran's claim for entitlements under Part 3 or 4 (whether for full entitlements or partial entitlements), VANZ must appoint a case manager.

prov.heading: Appointment of case manager and provision of entitlements before claim accepted

para.text: VANZ may appoint a case manager under section 112 and provide entitlements before accepting a veteran's claim in relation to entitlements under Part 4—
if reasonably satisfied that the veteran's injury or illness in respect of which the veteran's claim is made is service-related; but
only in relation to the provision of entitlements for rehabilitation.

prov.heading: VANZ's responsibilities in relation to rehabilitation plan

para.text: VANZ must—
ensure that a rehabilitation plan is prepared,—
in the case of a veteran who has been granted an entitlement under Part 3; or
in the case of a veteran who is claiming full entitlements under Part 4; and
liaise with ACC as to whether VANZ or ACC should prepare a rehabilitation plan, in the case of a veteran who is not claiming full entitlements under Part 3 or 4; and
if, under paragraph (b), it is decided that—
VANZ should prepare the rehabilitation plan, ensure a rehabilitation plan is prepared; or
ACC should prepare the rehabilitation plan, liaise with ACC about the rehabilitation plan that ACC is preparing.

prov.heading: General duty of VANZ

para.text: VANZ must take all reasonable and practicable steps to assist a veteran to achieve the maximum level of rehabilitation, having regard to the nature and extent of the veteran's disablement.
If, under section 114, ACC is to prepare a rehabilitation plan—
VANZ must act under subsection (1) in accordance with that plan; and
VANZ's obligations under this subpart apply in relation to rehabilitation not covered by the rehabilitation plan prepared by ACC.

prov.heading: VANZ to determine need for rehabilitation plan

para.text: Within 13 weeks after VANZ accepts a veteran's claim for entitlements under Part 3 or 4, VANZ—
must—
determine whether the veteran is likely to need social or vocational rehabilitation after the 13 weeks have ended; and
if so, prepare an individual rehabilitation plan in consultation with the veteran; and
may include in the plan provision for treatment.

prov.heading: Provision of rehabilitation before and after individual rehabilitation plan agreed

para.text: Before an individual rehabilitation plan for a veteran is agreed, VANZ is responsible for providing the veteran with—
social rehabilitation that VANZ considers suitable for the veteran and necessary in the circumstances, having regard to the purpose in section 120; and
vocational rehabilitation that VANZ considers suitable for the veteran and appropriate in the circumstances, having regard to the purpose in section 125.
VANZ may provide rehabilitation under subsection (1) before—
any assessment of the veteran is undertaken or completed for the purposes of this Part; or
starting or concluding its consideration of the matters specified in section 128(1).
To avoid doubt, subsections (1) and (2) do not prevent the provision of treatment before an individual rehabilitation plan is agreed.

After an individual rehabilitation plan for the veteran is agreed, VANZ is responsible for providing the veteran with rehabilitation in accordance with the plan and Schedule 2, but only to the extent that VANZ has specified which services it will provide under the plan.

prov.heading: Assessment of needs and content of individual rehabilitation plan

para.text: In preparing an individual rehabilitation plan, VANZ must assess the veteran's needs for rehabilitation, having regard to the purposes in sections 120 and 125.
An individual rehabilitation plan must—
identify the veteran's needs for rehabilitation; and

identify any support given to the veteran by the veteran’s spouse or partner, any child or dependant of the veteran, or any other person who provides non-professional support to the veteran, and the needs of any of those persons in relation to the support they give; and
identify the assessments to be done; and
identify services appropriate to those needs, whether or not VANZ is responsible for providing any or all of those services; and
specify which of the services identified under paragraph (c) VANZ will provide, pay for, or contribute to.
For the purposes of subsection (2)﻿(a), VANZ must assess a veteran's needs for—
social rehabilitation under section 124 and clauses 8 to 17 of Schedule 2; and
for vocational rehabilitation under sections 130 to 137.
However, VANZ is not required to assess a veteran's needs under subsection (3)﻿(b) if the veteran's needs are solely related to maintaining employment.

history.history-note: Section 118(2)﻿(aa): inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 21 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Individual rehabilitation plan must be updated

para.text: An individual rehabilitation plan must be updated from time to time to reflect the outcome of assessments done and progress made under the plan.
VANZ—
is responsible for ensuring subsection (1) is complied with if it is required under section 114 to prepare the rehabilitation plan:
must liaise with ACC about ACC complying with subsection (1) if ACC is required under section 114 to prepare the rehabilitation plan.

prov.heading: Purpose of social rehabilitation

para.text: The purpose of social rehabilitation is to assist in restoring a veteran's independence to the maximum extent practicable.

prov.heading: VANZ's responsibility to provide key aspects of social rehabilitation

para.text: In this section, key aspect of social rehabilitation means any of the following:
aids and appliances:
attendant care:
child care:
education support:
home help:
modifications to the home:
training for independence:
transport for independence.
Terms in subsection (1)﻿(a), (b), and (d) to (h) have the same meaning as in clause 7 of Schedule 2.
VANZ is responsible for providing a key aspect of social rehabilitation to a veteran—
if the conditions in subsection (4) are met; but
not earlier than a date determined in accordance with section 123.
The conditions are—
a veteran is assessed or reassessed under section 124 as needing the key aspect; and
the provision of the key aspect is in accordance with VANZ's assessment of it under whichever of clauses 8 to 16 of Schedule 2 are relevant; and
VANZ considers that the key aspect—
is required as a direct consequence of the injury or illness for which the veteran has entitlements under Part 3 or 4; and
is for the purpose set out in section 120; and
is necessary and appropriate, and of the quality required, for that purpose; and
is of a type normally provided by a rehabilitation provider; and
the provision of the key aspect has been agreed in the veteran's individual rehabilitation plan, if a plan has been agreed.
This section is subject to any regulations made under section 267.

prov.heading: VANZ may provide other social rehabilitation

para.text: VANZ may provide any other social rehabilitation if—
it is required as a direct consequence of the injury or illness for which the veteran has entitlements under this Part; and
the veteran is assessed or reassessed under section 124 as needing it; and
VANZ considers that it is—
for the purpose set out in section 120; and
necessary and appropriate, and of the quality required, for that purpose; and
of a type normally provided by a rehabilitation provider; and
its provision has been agreed in the veteran's individual rehabilitation plan, if a plan has been agreed.
This section is subject to any regulations made under sections 267 and 268.

prov.heading: Date of entitlement to social rehabilitation

para.text: This section applies to the provision of social rehabilitation that VANZ—
is responsible for providing under section 121; or
has decided to provide under section 122.
VANZ is responsible for providing social rehabilitation on the later of—
the date on which the application for the social rehabilitation is made; or
if the need for the social rehabilitation is assessed to arise after the date of the application, the date when it is assessed to arise.

However, if VANZ considers that, in the veteran's circumstances, it would be unreasonable for the veteran to have made an earlier application, it may begin providing social rehabilitation to the veteran on and from another date that it considers reasonable.
Subsection (3) does not entitle a veteran to entitlements that the veteran would not have been entitled to had the application been made earlier.

prov.heading: Assessment and reassessment of need for social rehabilitation

para.text: An assessment under this section assesses a veteran's need for social rehabilitation and identifies the specific social rehabilitation that the veteran needs.
Assessments and reassessments must be undertaken by assessors appointed under section 212.
A veteran's need for social rehabilitation—
may be reassessed from time to time; and
must be reassessed if VANZ considers that the veteran's condition or circumstances have changed.
The matters to be taken into account in an assessment or reassessment include—
the level of independence a veteran had before suffering the injury or illness:
the level of independence a veteran has after suffering the injury or illness:
the limitations suffered by a veteran as a result of the injury or illness:
the kinds of social rehabilitation that are appropriate for a veteran to minimise those limitations:
the rehabilitation outcome that would be achieved by providing particular social rehabilitation:
the alternatives and options available for providing particular social rehabilitation so as to achieve the relevant rehabilitation outcome in the most cost-effective way:
any social rehabilitation (not provided as vocational rehabilitation) that may reasonably be provided to enable a veteran who is entitled to vocational rehabilitation to participate in employment:
the geographical location in which a veteran lives:
in the case of a reassessment,—
whether any item that VANZ provided for the purposes of social rehabilitation is in such a condition as to need replacing:
changes in the veteran's condition or circumstances since the last assessment was undertaken.
VANZ must provide to an assessor all information VANZ has that is relevant to the assessment.

prov.heading: Purpose of vocational rehabilitation

para.text: The purpose of vocational rehabilitation is to help a veteran to, as appropriate,—
maintain employment; or
obtain employment; or
regain or acquire vocational independence.
Without limiting subsection (1), the provision of vocational rehabilitation includes the provision of activities for the purpose of maintaining or obtaining employment that is—
suitable for the veteran; and
appropriate for the veteran's levels of training and experience.

prov.heading: VANZ responsible for providing vocational rehabilitation

para.text: VANZ is responsible for providing vocational rehabilitation to a veteran who—
has suffered an injury or illness for which he or she has entitlements under Part 3 or 4; and
is—
entitled to weekly income compensation or weekly compensation; or
likely, unless he or she has vocational rehabilitation, to be entitled to weekly income compensation or weekly compensation; or
on parental leave.

Despite subsection (1)﻿(b)﻿(i), VANZ is responsible for providing vocational rehabilitation to a person who was entitled to weekly compensation and who would, but for section 104, continue to be entitled to weekly income compensation or weekly compensation.

prov.heading: Matters to be considered in deciding whether to provide vocational rehabilitation

para.text: In deciding whether to provide vocational rehabilitation, VANZ must have regard to the matters in section 128.
In deciding what vocational rehabilitation is appropriate for the veteran to achieve the purpose of vocational rehabilitation under section 125,—

VANZ must consider whether it is reasonably practicable to return the veteran to the same employment in which the veteran was engaged, and with the employer who was employing the veteran, when the veteran's incapacity commenced; and
if it is not, VANZ must consider the following matters:
whether it is reasonably practicable to return the veteran to an employment of a different kind with that employer:
whether it is reasonably practicable to return the veteran to the employment in which the veteran was engaged when the veteran's incapacity commenced, but with a different employer:
whether it is reasonably practicable to return the veteran to a different employment with a different employer, in which the veteran is able to use his or her experience, education, or training:
whether it is reasonably practicable to help the veteran use as many of his or her pre-injury skills as possible to obtain employment.

prov.heading: Further matters to be considered in deciding whether to provide vocational rehabilitation

para.text: In deciding whether to provide vocational rehabilitation, VANZ must have regard to—
whether the vocational rehabilitation is likely to achieve its purpose under the veteran's individual rehabilitation plan; and

whether the vocational rehabilitation is likely to be cost-effective, having regard to the likelihood that costs of entitlements under this Act will be reduced as a result of the provision of vocational rehabilitation; and
whether the vocational rehabilitation is appropriate in the circumstances.

VANZ must provide the vocational rehabilitation for the minimum period necessary to achieve its purpose, but must not provide any vocational rehabilitation for longer than 3 years (which need not be consecutive).
However, VANZ may, at its discretion, provide vocational rehabilitation for longer than 3 years if VANZ considers that—
the vocational rehabilitation would be likely to achieve its purpose under the veteran’s individual rehabilitation plan; and

the vocational rehabilitation would be likely to be cost-effective, having regard to the likelihood that costs of entitlements under this Act will be reduced as a result of the provision of vocational rehabilitation; and
the vocational rehabilitation would be appropriate in the circumstances.

Despite subsections (1)﻿(b) and (3)﻿(b), VANZ must not take into account as a factor against providing vocational rehabilitation that the veteran is, or may become, a person to whom section 65 or 104 (relationship between weekly compensation and New Zealand superannuation) applies.
This section is subject to any regulations made under section 267.

prov.heading: Vocational rehabilitation may start or resume if circumstances change

para.text: VANZ may, at any time, decide whether or not there has been a change of circumstances affecting the veteran's need for vocational rehabilitation.
If VANZ decides that there has been such a change, VANZ and the veteran may agree to the modification of the veteran's individual rehabilitation plan to reflect the changed circumstances.
VANZ may resume providing vocational rehabilitation under the veteran's individual rehabilitation plan, with any agreed modifications, to a veteran who—
had vocational rehabilitation; and
as a result, obtained employment; but
is unable to maintain the employment because of his or her incapacity.
This section is subject to section 128(2) and (3).

prov.heading: Assessment of veteran's vocational rehabilitation needs

para.text: An assessment of a veteran's vocational rehabilitation needs must consist of—
an initial occupational assessment to identify the types of work that may be appropriate for the veteran; and
an initial medical assessment to determine whether the types of work identified under paragraph (a) are, or are likely to be, medically sustainable for the veteran.

prov.heading: Occupational assessor

para.text: An occupational assessment must be undertaken by an assessor appointed under section 212 whom VANZ considers has the appropriate qualifications and experience to do the assessment required in the particular case.

prov.heading: Conduct of initial occupational assessment

para.text: An occupational assessor who undertakes an initial occupational assessment must—
take into account information provided by VANZ and the veteran; and
discuss with the veteran all the types of work that are available in New Zealand and suitable for the veteran; and
consider any comments the veteran makes to the assessor about those types of work.

In considering the suitability of the types of work referred to in subsection (1)﻿(b), the occupational assessor may take into account, among other things, the veteran’s earnings before the veteran’s incapacity.
VANZ must provide to an occupational assessor all information VANZ has that is relevant to an initial occupational assessment.

prov.heading: Report on initial occupational assessment

para.text: The occupational assessor must prepare and provide to VANZ a report on the initial occupational assessment.
The report must—
identify the types of work for the purposes of section 130(a); and
take into account the information, discussions, and comments referred to in section 132(1).
VANZ must provide a copy of the report to the veteran and the medical assessor.

prov.heading: Medical assessor

para.text: A medical assessment must be undertaken by a medical practitioner appointed under section 212 whom VANZ considers has the appropriate qualifications and experience to do the assessment in the particular case.

prov.heading: Assessments when medical assessor unavailable

para.text: A medical practitioner who does not qualify under section 212 may undertake a medical assessment if VANZ is satisfied that—
the circumstances in subsection (2) exist; and
the medical practitioner's qualifications and experience are broadly comparable with the qualifications and experience specified in section 212.
The circumstances are that—
a medical practitioner who does qualify under section 212 is not available to undertake a medical assessment without unreasonable delay or unreasonable inconvenience to the veteran; and
the delay or inconvenience would have an adverse effect on providing vocational rehabilitation to the veteran.
Sections 136 and 137 apply to a medical practitioner who qualifies under this section to undertake a medical assessment.

prov.heading: Conduct of initial medical assessment

para.text: A medical assessor who undertakes an initial medical assessment must take into account—
information provided to the assessor by VANZ; and
any of the following reports, information, or comments provided to the assessor:
medical reports requested by VANZ before the individual rehabilitation plan was prepared:
any other relevant medical reports; and
the report of the occupational assessor on the initial occupational assessment; and
the medical assessor's clinical examination of the veteran; and
any other information or comments that the veteran requests the medical assessor to take into account and that the medical assessor decides are relevant.
The medical assessor must also take into account any condition suffered by the veteran that is not related to the veteran's injury or illness.
VANZ must provide to a medical assessor all information VANZ has that is relevant to an initial medical assessment.

prov.heading: Report on initial medical assessment

para.text: The medical assessor must prepare and provide to VANZ a report on the initial medical assessment.
The report must—
contain the determination required by section 130(b); and
take into account the matters referred to in section 136.
VANZ must provide a copy of the report to the veteran.

prov.heading: VANZ to establish and administer veterans' independence programme

para.text: VANZ must establish and administer a veterans' independence programme (the programme) in accordance with this subpart.

prov.heading: Purpose of programme

para.text: The purpose of the programme is to provide services and support to veterans to the extent that they cannot undertake activities that are required in order for them to live independently in their home.

history.history-note: Section 139(2): repealed, on 1 October 2020, by section 22 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Scope of programme

para.text: Services and support under the programme are to be provided to a veteran with qualifying service to the extent assessed by VANZ and subject to any limits specified in regulations made under section 265.
Services and support to be provided under the programme include—
attendant care:
home help:
house and section maintenance:
home adaptations:
travel (other than transport related to treatment and rehabilitation), including an escort if a veteran is unable to travel alone:
allowance to contribute to cost of adaptive clothing and footwear.

prov.heading: Extent to which services and support to be provided under programme

para.text: A veteran who is entitled to impairment compensation under Part 3 or 4 is entitled to apply for all types of services and support provided under the programme.
For the purposes of subsection (1), the provision of services and support under the programme is to be determined in accordance with regulations made under section 265.

A veteran who is not entitled to impairment compensation under Part 3 or 4 may apply for all types of services and support provided under the programme, but the provision of services and support is at the discretion of VANZ.
In this section, impairment compensation means—
the disablement pension payable under subpart 3 of Part 3:
the independence allowance and lump sums payable under subpart 4 of Part 4.

prov.heading: Application for services and support

para.text: A veteran becomes entitled to services and support when—
the veteran has completed and provided to VANZ an application form provided by VANZ; and
the veteran has supplied, in the form required by VANZ, any supporting evidence or information required by VANZ; and
VANZ has accepted the application.
If an application is accepted by VANZ, the veteran's entitlement to the services and support applied for is to be treated as beginning on the day on which VANZ received the application.

prov.heading: Surviving spouse or partner

para.text: This section applies to the surviving spouse or partner of a deceased veteran, if the veteran was receiving or was entitled to services and support under the programme before his or her death.
For the period of 12 months after the veteran's death, his or her spouse or partner is entitled to services and support under the programme to the same extent as the veteran was entitled to.
Subsection (2) does not apply to a spouse or partner who is receiving services and support under section 143A.

history.history-note: Section 143(3): replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 23 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 143(4): repealed, on 1 October 2020, by section 23 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.label: 143A
prov.heading: Spouse or partner of veteran in long-term residential care

para.text: This section applies to the spouse or partner of a veteran who—
is receiving or is entitled to services and support under the programme; and
goes into long-term residential care.

The veteran’s spouse or partner is entitled to services and support under the programme, to the same extent as the veteran was entitled, for 12 months after the veteran goes into long-term residential care.
The veteran’s spouse or partner continues to be entitled to services and support under subsection (2) if the veteran dies within 12 months after going into long-term residential care.

history.history-note: Section 143A: inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 24 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Regulations

para.text: Regulations may be made under section 265 that specify—
the services and support provided under the programme:
the criteria for the purposes of section 141(2):
the amounts that may be paid under the programme:
the circumstances in which and the method by which payments are to be made:
the persons to whom payments will be made:
different levels of services or support according to the level of impairment or need:
whether assessments of impairment or need are required and, if so, the process for conducting the assessments:
any other matters necessary or desirable for the effective and efficient administration of the programme.

subpart.heading: Regulations relating to aids and appliances

prov.heading: Regulations relating to aids and appliances

para.text: Regulations may be made under section 265 that do either or both of the following:
specify limits on the cost of providing or contributing to the cost of providing aids and appliances; and
provide for the payment of a weekly battery allowance payable to veterans using hearing aids approved by VANZ.

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this subpart, motor vehicle—
includes a mobility device as defined in section 2(1) of the Land Transport Act 1998; but
does not include a vehicle that the director has declared is not a motor vehicle under section 168A of the Land Transport Act 1998.

prov.heading: Eligibility for motor vehicle grants

para.text: A veteran with total lower body mobility impairment or with severe lower body mobility impairment may apply to VANZ for a grant to be applied to the cost of a motor vehicle owned or to be purchased by the veteran for his or her personal transport.

prov.heading: Regulations relating to motor vehicle grants

para.text: Regulations may be made under section 265 specifying—
definitions of total lower body mobility impairment and severe lower body mobility impairment:
the criteria and process for determining whether a grant should be made, including (but not limited to) assessments and the number of occasions on which a grant can be made:
the maximum amount of a grant, including specifying different maximums in different circumstances:
any other matter for the effective and efficient administration of this subpart.
Without limiting subsection (1), if the regulations—
specify the number of occasions on which a grant can be made to a veteran, the regulations may specify that a motor vehicle loan under the War Pensions Act 1954 may be taken into account:
specify a minimum interval between grants, the regulations may specify that a motor vehicle loan under the War Pensions Act 1954 may be taken into account.
Despite regulations made for the purposes of this subpart, a veteran—

whose total lower body mobility impairment met the criteria for a motor vehicle loan under the War Pensions Act 1954 must be treated as meeting the criteria for total lower body mobility impairment for a motor vehicle grant under this subpart:

whose severe lower body mobility impairment met the criteria for a motor vehicle loan under the War Pensions Act 1954 must be treated as meeting the criteria for severe lower body mobility impairment for a motor vehicle grant under this subpart.

prov.heading: Retirement lump sum

para.text: A veteran is entitled, on reaching the New Zealand superannuation qualification age, to a retirement lump sum if—

the veteran received weekly income compensation under Part 3 or weekly compensation under Part 4 over a 10-year period before the date on which he or she reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age; and
an assessment under section 150 determines that the value of the veteran’s non-exempt assets is less than the applicable asset threshold.
To avoid doubt,—
this section applies only to veterans who reach the New Zealand superannuation qualification age on or after the commencement of this section; and
the 10-year period referred to in subsection (1) does not have to be a continuous period.
In this section, weekly income compensation includes the veteran’s pension, war veteran’s allowance, war service pension, and economic pension payable under the War Pensions Act 1954.

prov.heading: Asset assessment

para.text: A veteran who applies for a retirement lump sum must also apply for an asset assessment.
An application for an asset assessment must be made on a form provided for the purpose by VANZ.
VANZ must arrange for an asset assessment to be conducted as soon as practicable after receiving an application.
The value of the veteran’s assets is to be assessed as at the date the veteran reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age.
An asset assessment must be conducted in accordance with regulations made under section 265.

prov.heading: Regulations relating to this subpart

para.text: Regulations may be made under section 265 on the recommendation of the Minister that specify for the purposes of this subpart—
the amount of the retirement lump sum payment; and
the types of assets to be taken into account in an asset assessment; and
what assets are exempt from being taken into account in an assessment; and
the asset threshold; and
how an asset assessment is to be conducted.
For the purposes of subsection (1)﻿(d),—
more than 1 asset threshold may be specified for different circumstances; and
a veteran may be permitted to elect which threshold applies to him or her.

prov.heading: Application

para.text: Section 153 applies in relation to the death of a veteran if—
the veteran had undertaken qualifying operational service; or
the veteran’s death was a service-related death.
Section 154 applies to the death of other veterans (Scheme One only).

history.history-note: Section 152: replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 25 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Funeral expenses: veterans with qualifying operational service or service-related death

para.text: VANZ may pay or contribute to the payment of the expenses of the funeral and burial or cremation of the veteran.
The maximum amount payable under subsection (1) must be specified in regulations made under section 265.

If the veteran dies in hospital and his or her burial or cremation is to take place in the locality where the veteran was admitted to hospital, VANZ is responsible for paying the reasonable cost of transporting the body.
If the veteran dies in hospital and his or her burial or cremation is to take place outside the locality where the veteran was admitted to hospital, VANZ is responsible for paying the lesser of—
the reasonable cost of transporting the body to the place of burial or cremation; or
an amount equivalent to the reasonable cost of transporting the body to the locality from where the veteran was admitted to hospital.
Regulations may be made under section 265 specifying the maximum amount that may be paid under subsection (3) or (4) for the cost of transporting a veteran's body.

history.history-note: Section 153 heading: replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 26 of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Funeral expenses: other deceased veterans (Scheme One only)

para.text: VANZ may pay a reasonable amount in respect of the funeral and burial or cremation of a veteran to whom section 153 does not apply if subsection (2) applies.
This subsection applies if—
the veteran was, at the time of death, receiving any of the following pensions or entitlements:
weekly income compensation under Part 3:
veteran's pension under Part 6 of this Act or Part 6 of the War Pensions Act 1954:
New Zealand Superannuation:
a supported living payment under the Social Security Act 2018; and
the veteran leaves a surviving spouse or partner, or a child or children, or dependants, who will, in the opinion of VANZ be entitled to—
a pension under section 66, 72, or 76.
The maximum amount payable under subsection (1) must be specified in regulations made under section 265.

history.history-note: Section 154 heading: replaced, on 1 October 2020, by section 27(1) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 154(1): amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 27(2) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 154(2)﻿(a)﻿(ii): repealed, on 1 October 2020, by section 27(3) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 154(2)﻿(a)﻿(v): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 154(2)﻿(b)﻿(ii): repealed, on 1 October 2020, by section 27(3) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Extent to which funeral expenses payable under this subpart and under Accident Compensation Act 2001

para.text: Funeral expenses are payable under this subpart in respect of a veteran in addition to any funeral grant payable under the Accident Compensation Act 2001.

However, if the funeral expenses payable under this subpart in respect of a veteran are more than a funeral grant payable under the Accident Compensation Act 2001, VANZ is required to pay only the difference.
In this section, funeral expenses does not include the costs referred to in section 153(3) or (4), as the case may be.

prov.heading: Plaques and headstones

para.text: VANZ is responsible for providing, or meeting the cost of providing, plaques and headstones in relation to the burial of veterans and their spouses and partners if—
the burials take place in cemeteries or parts of cemeteries under section 15 of the Burial and Cremation Act 1964; or
the burials do not take place under that section, but would have been eligible to take place under that section.
For the purposes of subsection (1), burial includes the burial of cremated remains.
Regulations may be made under section 265 specifying the circumstances and extent to which VANZ is responsible for providing or meeting the cost of providing plaques and headstones.

prov.heading: Veteran entitled to financial advice in certain circumstances

para.text: This section applies—
in relation to the following payments:
lump sum payments payable under section 98:
aggregated payments payable under clause 55 of Schedule 2:
any other payment under this Act that is specified in regulations made under section 265 for the purposes of this section; and
if the payment is equal to or greater than the prescribed amount.

VANZ must provide or meet the cost of providing the veteran with financial advice from a financial advice provider under the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 for the purpose of enabling the veteran to make an informed decision about investing the lump sum.
Regulations may be made under section 265 that specify—
any other payment under this Act for the purposes of this section:
the prescribed amount for the purposes of subsection (1)﻿(b):
any limits on the extent to which VANZ provides or meets the cost of providing financial advice:
whether VANZ will arrange for the provision of financial advice in all cases or meet the costs of financial advice in all cases or a combination of both.
To avoid doubt, VANZ is under no liability for advice provided by a financial advice provider under this section.

history.history-note: Section 157(2): amended, on 15 March 2021, by section 98 of the Financial Services Legislation Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 8).
Section 157(4): amended, on 15 March 2021, by section 98 of the Financial Services Legislation Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 8).

part.heading: Veteran's pension

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires,—
dependent child, in relation to any person, means a child—
whose care is primarily the responsibility of that person; and
who is being maintained as a member of that person’s family; and
who is financially dependent on that person; and
who is not a child in respect of whom payments are being made under section 363 of the Oranga Tamariki Act 1989
employment income—

means any salary, wage, allowance, bonus, gratuity, extra salary, compensation for loss of office or employment, emolument (of whatever kind), or other benefit in money, for, or in connection with, the employment or service of the person; and
includes,—
subject to paragraph (c), any expenditure on account of an employee (within the meaning of section YA 1 of the Income Tax Act 2007); and
any benefit of a kind referred to in section CE 1(1)﻿(d) of the Income Tax Act 2007; and

in the case of a person who, in the relevant period, has been provided, in respect of any office or position held by the person, with board or lodging (or the use of a house or quarters) or has been paid an allowance instead of being provided with board or lodging (or with the use of a house or quarters), the value of those benefits (and the value of the benefits is to be determined, in the case of a dispute, by VANZ); but
does not include any employer’s superannuation contribution (within the meaning of section YA 1 of the Income Tax Act 2007)
full-time employment has the same meaning as in Schedule 2 of the Social Security Act 2018
income has the same meaning as in Schedule 2 of the Social Security Act 2018
living alone, in relation to a single person, has the meaning given to it by section 159
New Zealand superannuation means New Zealand superannuation paid or payable under the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001
ordinarily resident, in relation to any person, does not include being unlawfully resident in New Zealand
relationship (partner not receiving superannuation or pension) rate means the rate of a veteran’s pension specified in regulations that is payable to a person—
who is married or in a civil union or in a de facto relationship; and
whose spouse or partner is not entitled to receive New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension
relationship rate means the rate of a veteran’s pension specified in regulations that is payable to a person who is married or in a civil union or in a de facto relationship
single means not married nor in a civil union nor in a de facto relationship
single living alone rate means the rate of a veteran's pension specified in regulations that is payable to a single person who is living alone
single sharing accommodation rate means the rate of a veteran's pension specified in regulations that is payable to a single person who is not living alone
spouse means the husband or wife of an applicant or person receiving a veteran’s pension, as the case may require.
Unless the context otherwise requires,—
any word or expression used in this Part but not defined in this Part has the same meaning as it has in section 7 of this Act; and
any word or expression used in this Part but not defined in this Part or in section 7 of this Act has the meaning given to it in Schedule 2 of the Social Security Act 2018.

history.history-note: Section 158(1) dependent child paragraph (d): amended, on 14 July 2017, by section 149 of the Children, Young Persons, and Their Families (Oranga Tamariki) Legislation Act 2017 (2017 No 31).
Section 158(1) full-time employment: amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 158(1) income: amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 158(1) Income Test 3: repealed, on 1 April 2020, by section 18 of the Social Assistance Legislation (Budget 2019 Welfare Package) Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 23).

Section 158(1) relationship (partner not receiving superannuation or pension) legacy rate: repealed, on 9 November 2020, by section 25 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 158(2)﻿(b): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.label: 158A
prov.heading: Relationship of this Part with Social Security Act 2018

para.text: The Social Security Act 2018 contains provisions relating to the administration, and other aspects, of the veteran’s pension.

history.history-note: Section 158A: inserted, on 9 November 2020, by section 26 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

prov.heading: Meaning of living alone

para.text: A single person (person A) is living alone only if he or she meets the 2 conditions specified in subsections (2) and (3).
The first condition is that person A occupies any of the following kinds of accommodation as person A’s principal residence:
a house or flat:
a boat or craft moored within the following sea or waters (whether or not it travels within that sea or any of those waters from time to time):
the territorial sea of New Zealand or any internal waters of New Zealand:
any waters within New Zealand that are a lake, estuary, lagoon, river, stream, creek, or other waters:
a hotel room or motel unit:
a room in a boarding house:
a unit of accommodation in a caravan park:
a mobile home (whether or not it is in a caravan park and whether or not it travels from time to time) that VANZ is satisfied is sufficiently self-contained to be reasonably habitable as a residence:
any other accommodation of a kind prescribed by regulations made under section 265.
The second condition is that person A does not share that residence with any person 18 years old or older, except a person to whom subsection (4) or (5) applies.
This subsection applies to person A's dependent child—
who—
is 18 years old (despite the definition of child in Schedule 2 of the Social Security Act 2018); and
is not financially independent; and
is attending a school or a tertiary educational establishment; but
only until the close of 31 December in the year in which the child turns 18 years old.
This subsection applies to a temporary visitor to person A's residence who stays there less than 13 weeks in any period of 26 weeks.
In this section,—
boarding house has the same meaning as in section 66B of the Residential Tenancies Act 1986
territorial sea of New Zealand and internal waters of New Zealand have the same meanings as in the Territorial Sea, Contiguous Zone, and Exclusive Economic Zone Act 1977.

history.history-note: Section 159(2): replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 27(1) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 159(4)﻿(a)﻿(i): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 159(6): repealed, on 9 November 2020, by section 27(2) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

Section 159(7) internal waters of New Zealand (previously internal waters): amended, on 9 November 2020, by section 27(3) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

prov.heading: Delegation of general manager's functions, duties, and powers under this Part

para.text: The general manager may, either generally or particularly, delegate to the chief executive of the department for the time being responsible for the administration of the Social Security Act 2018 any of the general manager’s functions, duties, and powers under—
this Part; and
any provision in the Social Security Act 2018 that relates to the veteran's pension.
Subsection (1) applies to any of the functions, duties, and powers that are the general manager's functions, duties, and powers in accordance with section 201.
Clauses 2 to 4 of Schedule 6 of the Public Service Act 2020 apply to a delegation under subsection (1) as if the delegation were made under clause 2 of that schedule.

history.history-note: Section 160(1): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 160(1)﻿(b): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 160(3): replaced, on 7 August 2020, by section 135 of the Public Service Act 2020 (2020 No 40).

prov.heading: Entitlement to veteran’s pension

para.text: A veteran is entitled to a veteran’s pension if—
the veteran has qualifying operational service; and
the veteran has reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age and is eligible to receive New Zealand superannuation.
A person is entitled to a veteran's pension if—
the person has reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age and is eligible to receive New Zealand superannuation; and
the person was, immediately before the commencement of this Part, entitled to receive a veteran’s pension under the War Pensions Act 1954.
This section is subject to section 167.

prov.heading: Entitlement of spouse or partner of veteran to veteran's pension

para.text: For the purposes of subsection (2)﻿(b), this subsection applies to a veteran if—
the veteran has qualifying operational service; and
the veteran has reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age and is eligible to receive New Zealand superannuation; and
either—
the veteran is entitled to a disablement pension or an independence allowance, or has been paid a lump sum under section 98, for whole-person impairment of 52% or more; or
the veteran was, immediately before the commencement of this Part, entitled to a war disablement pension for disablement of 70% or more under Part 2, 4, or 5 of the War Pensions Act 1954.
Subject to subsection (3), the spouse or partner of a veteran is entitled to a veteran’s pension if—
the spouse or partner has reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age and is eligible to receive New Zealand superannuation; and
subsection (1) applies to the veteran or the veteran is entitled to a veteran’s pension under section 164.
A spouse or partner described in subsection (2) is not entitled to a veteran's pension if—
the veteran and the claimant are living apart or are not maintaining a relationship in the nature of a marriage; and
the claimant is not contributing to the veteran's day-to-day welfare.

Despite subsection (3), a spouse or partner described in subsection (2) is entitled to a veteran's pension if the circumstances described in subsection (3) occurred principally because of the health, imprisonment, or employment obligations of the veteran or the claimant.
This section is subject to section 167.

prov.heading: Entitlement of spouse or partner of deceased veteran to veteran's pension

para.text: The spouse or partner of a deceased veteran is entitled to a veteran’s pension if—
the spouse or partner has reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age and is eligible to receive New Zealand superannuation; and
the veteran had qualifying operational service; and
the veteran, had he or she not died, would have reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age and been eligible to receive New Zealand superannuation; and
one of the following applies:

the veteran was, at the date of his or her death, receiving a disablement pension or an independence allowance, or had been paid a lump sum under section 98, for whole-person impairment of 52% or more:
the veteran was, at the date of his or her death, receiving a war disablement pension for disablement of 70% or more under Part 2, 4, or 5 of the War Pensions Act 1954:
VANZ considers that, had the veteran not died, the veteran would have been eligible to receive,—
if the veteran died after the commencement of this Part, a disablement pension or an independence allowance, or a lump sum under section 98, for whole-person impairment of 52% or more; or
if the veteran died before the commencement of this Part, a war disablement pension for disablement of 70% or more under Part 2, 4, or 5 of the War Pensions Act 1954.
The spouse or partner of a deceased veteran is entitled to a veteran’s pension if—
the spouse or partner has reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age and is eligible to receive New Zealand superannuation; and
the veteran had qualifying operational service; and
the veteran died—
before the commencement of this Part and either—
the veteran was, at the date of his or her death, receiving a veteran's pension under section 70(1)﻿(b)﻿(ii) of the War Pensions Act 1954; or
VANZ considers that the veteran would have been eligible, at the date of his or her death, to receive a veteran's pension under section 70(1)﻿(b)﻿(ii) of the War Pensions Act 1954; or
after the commencement of this Part and the veteran was, at the date of his or her death, entitled to a veteran's pension under section 164.
Subsections (1) and (2) do not apply if, immediately before the veteran's death,—
the veteran and the claimant were living apart or were not maintaining a relationship in the nature of marriage; and
the claimant was not contributing to the veteran's day-to-day welfare.
Subsection (3) does not apply if the circumstances described in that subsection occurred principally because of the health, imprisonment, or employment obligations of the veteran or the claimant.
If a spouse or partner described in subsection (1) or (2) enters into a new relationship after the veteran's death, the spouse or partner's entitlement to the veteran's pension ceases immediately.
For the purposes of subsection (5), a spouse or partner of a deceased veteran enters into a new relationship after the veteran's death, when the spouse or partner—
marries; or
enters into a civil union; or
enters into a de facto relationship.
This section is subject to section 167.

part.crosshead: Entitlement to veteran's pension by reason of infirmity

prov.heading: Entitlement to veteran’s pension under War Pensions Act 1954 by reason of infirmity

para.text: A veteran is entitled to a veteran's pension if, at the commencement of this Part, he or she—
was receiving a veteran’s pension under the War Pensions Act 1954 by reason of infirmity in accordance with section 70(1)﻿(b)﻿(ii) of that Act; and
has not reached the New Zealand superannuation qualification age.
The veteran ceases to be entitled to a veteran's pension under subsection (1) on reaching the New Zealand superannuation qualification age.
If the veteran is also entitled to weekly income compensation under subpart 4 of Part 3, the veteran may elect to receive weekly income compensation instead of a veteran’s pension.
If the veteran elects to receive weekly income compensation, all of the provisions of subpart 4 of Part 3 apply to the veteran.
A veteran who has received weekly income compensation under subpart 4 of Part 3 may not elect to receive a veteran's pension under this section.

prov.heading: Veteran’s ability to earn employment income

para.text: This section applies to a veteran who is receiving a veteran’s pension under section 164(1).
The veteran does not cease to be entitled to a veteran’s pension merely because he or she, at any time, is no longer prevented from undertaking employment because of a mental or physical infirmity.

However, if a medical practitioner or nurse practitioner certifies in accordance with section 166(2) that the veteran is not prevented from undertaking full-time employment as a result of a mental or physical infirmity, the veteran's entitlement to a veteran's pension under section 164 ceases.

prov.heading: Medical examination of veteran receiving pension under section 164

para.text: VANZ may require a veteran receiving a veteran’s pension under section 164 to undergo a medical examination by a medical practitioner or nurse practitioner appointed under section 212.
The medical practitioner or nurse practitioner must certify—
whether, in his or her opinion, the veteran is prevented from undertaking full-time employment as a result of a mental or physical infirmity; and
the estimated duration of that condition.
The medical practitioner or nurse practitioner must state the grounds for his or her opinion.

part.crosshead: Person may not receive New Zealand superannuation and veteran's pension

prov.heading: Person may not receive New Zealand superannuation and veteran's pension at same time

para.text: A person who is entitled to both a veteran’s pension and New Zealand superannuation may receive only one of those entitlements at any time.

A person who receives New Zealand superannuation and who would also, except for subsection (1), be entitled to a veteran's pension, may elect to receive a veteran's pension instead of New Zealand superannuation.

A person who receives a veteran's pension and who would also, except for subsection (1), be entitled to New Zealand superannuation, may elect to receive New Zealand superannuation instead of a veteran's pension.

part.crosshead: Commencement and rates of veteran's pension

prov.heading: Commencement of veteran’s pension

para.text: A veteran’s pension commences on the later of—
the date on which the applicant became entitled to it; and
the date on which VANZ received the application for it.

prov.heading: Rates of veteran's pension

para.text: The rate of veteran's pension payable to a person is the appropriate rate specified in regulations made under section 265.
The following rates of veteran's pension must be specified in the regulations referred to in subsection (1):
a single living alone rate:
a single sharing accommodation rate:
a relationship rate:
a relationship (partner not receiving superannuation or pension) rate.

The relationship (partner not receiving superannuation or pension) rate is the appropriate rate of veteran’s pension payable to a person only in the circumstances specified in clause 22 of Schedule 1.

history.history-note: Section 169(2)﻿(e): repealed, on 9 November 2020, by section 28(1) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 169(3): replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 28(2) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

prov.heading: Annual adjustment of rates of veteran's pension

para.text: The rates of veteran's pension must be reviewed in accordance with this section before 1 April every year and, if the review concludes that any of the rates require adjustment under this section, the relevant rate or rates must be adjusted as at 1 April by a notice published by VANZ in accordance with this section.
In this section,—
