average wage means the average ordinary time weekly earnings (males and females combined) as determined by the last QES before 1 March in each year (after the deduction of standard tax and the earner levy payable on those earnings)

Consumers Price Index (all groups) means the Consumers Price Index (all groups) published by Statistics New Zealand or, if that index ceases to be published, any measure certified by the Government Statistician as being equivalent to that index
earner levy means the levy payable under section 219(1) of the Accident Compensation Act 2001

standard tax means the amount of tax, calculated on a weekly basis, that would be deductible in accordance with tax code M specified in schedule 5, part A, clause 4, table row 1 of the Tax Administration Act 1994.

Subject to subsections (5) and (6), the relationship rate, the single living alone rate, and the single sharing accommodation rate must be adjusted upwards to reflect any percentage movement upwards in the Consumers Price Index (all groups) between—
the Consumers Price Index (all groups) for the quarter that ends on 31 December 1 year before the immediately preceding 31 December; and
the Consumers Price Index (all groups) for the quarter that ends on the immediately preceding 31 December.

The adjustments (by any percentage movements upwards in the CPI) required under subsection (3) as at 1 April in any year from 2015 to 2021 (inclusive) must, despite subsection (3), be calculated using index numbers of the Consumers Price Index less cigarettes and tobacco subgroup published by Statistics New Zealand or, if that index ceases to be published, any measure certified by the Government Statistician as being equivalent to that index.

If the relationship rate, the single living alone rate, or the single sharing accommodation rate does not meet the relevant criteria described in subsection (6), the rate must be adjusted to meet the criteria.
The criteria are—

the total amount payable to a veteran and the veteran's spouse or partner who are both eligible to receive a veteran's pension at the relationship rate (before any abatement under section 171 but after the deduction of standard tax) must not be less than 66% of the average wage or more than 72.5% of the average wage:

the single living alone rate (before any abatement under section 171 but after the deduction of standard tax) must be 65% of the total amount payable to a veteran and the veteran's spouse or partner who are both eligible to receive a veteran's pension paid at the relationship rate (before any abatement under section 171 but after the deduction of standard tax):

the single sharing accommodation rate (before any abatement under section 171 but after the deduction of standard tax) must be 60% of the total amount payable to a veteran and the veteran's spouse or partner who are both eligible to receive a veteran's pension paid at the relationship rate (before any abatement under section 171 but after the deduction of standard tax).
For the purposes of subsection (1), the notice—

must preserve the proportional relationship of the relationship (partner not receiving superannuation or pension) rate (before any abatement under section 171, but after the deduction of standard tax) to the relationship rate, the single living alone rate, and the single sharing accommodation rate (as they were before the adjustment):
must not reduce any of the rates of veteran's pension:
is secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements):

comes into force, or is to be treated as having come into force, on 1 April of the calendar year in which it is made, and applies to veterans’ pensions on and after that date, even if it is not yet published:
must, in addition to being published under the Legislation Act 2019, be published—
on an Internet site maintained by or on behalf of VANZ; and
in any other manner that VANZ thinks appropriate to bring the adjustments to the attention of claimants and the general public.

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: The maker must comply with subsection (7)﻿(d)
It is not required to be presented to the House of Representatives because a transitional exemption applies under Schedule 1 of the Legislation Act 2019
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

Section 170(2) CPI: repealed, on 1 September 2022, by section 107(1) of the Data and Statistics Act 2022 (2022 No 39).

Section 170(3)﻿(a): amended, on 1 September 2022, by section 107(1) of the Data and Statistics Act 2022 (2022 No 39).
Section 170(3)﻿(b): amended, on 1 September 2022, by section 107(1) of the Data and Statistics Act 2022 (2022 No 39).
Section 170(4): amended, on 1 September 2022, by section 107(1) of the Data and Statistics Act 2022 (2022 No 39).

Section 170(7)﻿(d): inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 28(3) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 170(7)﻿(d): amended, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.heading: Abatement of veteran’s pension

para.text: The amount payable to a veteran who is entitled to a veteran’s pension under section 161 or 164 is subject to abatement in accordance with regulations made under section 265.

Regulations made under section 265 may require different abatement (for example, different income tests, abatement rates, and abatement thresholds (for example, amounts, ranges, or both, of income per week)) for—
a veteran’s pension under section 161 and a veteran’s pension under section 164:
a veteran’s pension under section 164 if the veteran receives the veteran’s pension at—
the relationship rate, the single living alone rate, or the single sharing accommodation rate:
the relationship (partner not receiving superannuation or pension) rate.
Regulations made under section 265 that increase an amount of an abatement threshold (prescribed by regulations of that kind) for abatement of a veteran’s pension under section 161 or 164—
must state the date on which they are to have effect (which may be a date before the date on which they were made); and
apply to abatement thresholds as applicable on or after the date on which the regulations have effect.
Subsection (3) does not authorise any payment to be made before the date on which the regulations that increase the abatement threshold are made.

history.history-note: Section 171: replaced, on 1 April 2020, by section 19 of the Social Assistance Legislation (Budget 2019 Welfare Package) Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 23).
Section 171(2)﻿(b)﻿(ii): amended, on 9 November 2020, by section 30 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

prov.heading: Election if spouse or partner not entitled to superannuation or veteran's pension

history.history-note: Section 172: repealed, on 9 November 2020, by section 31 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

prov.heading: Entitlement to veteran's pension for spouses or partners of persons in long-term residential care

para.text: This section applies to any person—

who is not in long-term residential care but whose spouse or partner is in long-term residential care, whether or not the spouse or partner is a resident assessed as requiring care (within the meaning of section 5 of the Residential Care and Disability Support Services Act 2018); and
who is receiving a veteran’s pension, or whose spouse or partner is receiving a veteran’s pension.
A person to whom this section applies is entitled to a veteran's pension at—
the single living alone rate, if the person is living alone; or
the single sharing accommodation rate, if the person is not living alone.

Any benefit payable to a person to whom this section applies is not subject to abatement in respect of any income of the person that has been included in a means assessment of the person’s spouse or partner under the Residential Care and Disability Support Services Act 2018.
In subsection (3), any benefit,—
has, under section 158(2)﻿(b) of this Act, the meaning given to it in Schedule 2 of the Social Security Act 2018; and so
includes, for example, an accommodation supplement (as is explained in the guide in section 17A of that Act).

history.history-note: Section 173 heading: amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 29(1) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 173(1)﻿(a): amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 29(2) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 173(1)﻿(a): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 173(3): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 173(4): inserted, on 26 November 2022, by section 11 of the Social Security (Accommodation Supplement) Amendment Act 2022 (2022 No 65).

prov.heading: Lump sum payment on death

para.text: Subsection (2) applies on the death of the following:
a veteran to whom section 162(1) applies and who, on the date of death,—
is ordinarily resident in New Zealand; and
is receiving a veteran’s pension; and
a veteran who, on the date of death,—
is ordinarily resident in New Zealand; and
is receiving a veteran’s pension under section 164.
The amount specified in regulations made under section 265—
is payable to the surviving spouse or partner of the veteran; or
if there is no surviving spouse or partner, is to be apportioned equally between any dependent children of the veteran.
Subsection (4) applies on the death of the spouse or partner of a veteran if, on the date of death, the spouse or partner is—
ordinarily resident in New Zealand; and
receiving a veteran’s pension under section 162(2).
The amount specified in regulations made under section 265 is payable to the surviving veteran.
The regulations specifying the amounts payable under this section may provide for different amounts to be payable—
on the death of a veteran who,—
on the date of his or her death, was receiving a veteran’s pension on account of his or her own service; and
before 1 April 1990, was receiving a war veteran’s allowance under the War Pensions Act 1954:
on the death of a veteran who, on the date of his or her death, was receiving a veteran’s pension on account of his or her own service:
on the death of the spouse or partner of a veteran if the veteran is receiving a veteran’s pension on account of his or her own service and the spouse or partner was receiving a veteran’s pension.
The amount of any veteran’s pension paid or payable in respect of the person who has died following their death is deductable from any amount payable under this section.

part.crosshead: Review of decision relating to veteran's pension

prov.heading: Review of decision relating to veteran's pension

para.text: A person affected by a decision of VANZ under this Part may (subject to subsection (2)) appeal the decision—
to the social security appeal authority, in accordance with section 397(1)﻿(e) of the Social Security Act 2018; or
if the decision was made on medical grounds, to a board appointed under Schedule 9 of that Act.

A person may not appeal to the Social Security Appeal Authority a decision to decline entitlement to a veteran's pension on the basis that the service on which a claim to entitlement is based is not qualifying operational service.
A person affected by a decision described in subsection (2) may apply for a review of the decision in accordance with subpart 2 of Part 7.

history.history-note: Section 175(1): replaced, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: Effect of absence from New Zealand on veteran’s pension

para.text: A person is not entitled to a veteran’s pension while that person is absent from New Zealand, except as provided in sections 177 to 196 or in any reciprocity agreement adopted under section 380 of the Social Security Act 2018.

history.history-note: Section 176: amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: First 26 weeks of certain temporary absences

para.text: A veteran’s pension that would otherwise be payable to a person (other than a person who is receiving a veteran's pension overseas under section 181) is payable in respect of the first 26 weeks of any absence from New Zealand if—
the person’s absence does not exceed 30 weeks; or

the person’s absence exceeds 30 weeks and VANZ is satisfied that the absence beyond 30 weeks is due to circumstances beyond that person’s control that he or she could not reasonably have foreseen before departure.

prov.heading: Absences up to 2 years for medical treatment

para.text: VANZ may, in its discretion, pay a veteran’s pension to any person who would otherwise be entitled to receive it, but who is absent from New Zealand for any period or periods not exceeding 2 years in total because that person or his or her spouse, partner, child, or sibling is receiving medical treatment overseas for which the Ministry of Health is granting assistance.

prov.heading: Absences up to 156 weeks with aid agencies

para.text: VANZ may pay a veteran’s pension to any person who—
is otherwise entitled to receive it; and
is absent from New Zealand for a period not exceeding 156 weeks; and
is engaged, while absent from New Zealand, in full-time voluntary and unpaid humanitarian work for a recognised aid agency that—
has, as its principal function, the giving of aid and assistance to less advantaged communities in 1 or more other countries; and
has not deprived another person of paid employment to engage the person to do that work on an unpaid basis.
The chief executive of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade must determine any question that arises as to whether an aid agency fulfils the qualifications set out in subsection (1).

prov.heading: Date absence commences

para.text: If a person who is absent from New Zealand would have become entitled to a veteran’s pension during his or her absence but for section 176, the person’s absence must be regarded as having commenced on the day that he or she would have become entitled to a veteran’s pension.

prov.heading: Payment overseas of veteran's pension

para.text: Section 182—

applies to a country if it is not a country with whose government New Zealand has a reciprocity agreement in force under section 380 of the Social Security Act 2018, that relates to the veteran's pension; and
applies to a person if he or she has left New Zealand at a time when he or she—

was intending to reside for a period longer than 26 weeks in a country (or 2 or more countries) to which this subsection applies, but not intending to reside for a period longer than 52 weeks in a specified Pacific country (within the meaning of section 190(1)); or
was intending to travel for a period longer than 26 weeks, but not intending to reside in any country other than New Zealand.

history.history-note: Section 181(a): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: Entitlement to payments overseas

para.text: A person to whom this section applies is entitled to be paid a veteran's pension at the appropriate rate specified in section 183(1),—

in the case of a person who has left New Zealand at a time when he or she was intending to reside for a period longer than 26 weeks in a country (or 2 or more countries) to which this section applies, but not intending to reside for a period longer than 52 weeks in a specified Pacific country (within the meaning of section 190(1)), until he or she—
begins to reside in a country that is not a country to which this section applies; or
begins to reside in New Zealand again; or
begins to receive a veteran's pension otherwise than under this section; or
begins to receive New Zealand superannuation under the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001; and

in the case of a person who has left New Zealand at a time when he or she was intending to travel for a period longer than 26 weeks, but not intending to reside in any country other than New Zealand, until he or she—
begins to reside in a country that is not a country to which this section applies; or
returns to New Zealand.
For the purposes only of subsection (1)﻿(b)﻿(ii), a person does not return to New Zealand if—
he or she—

interrupts his or her overseas travel, and travels to and stays briefly in New Zealand, in order to attend an event (for example, a wedding or funeral) or to visit a person (for example, a sick or injured family member); and then
resumes his or her overseas travel; and
he or she does not while in New Zealand—
begin to receive a veteran's pension otherwise than under this section; or
begin to receive New Zealand superannuation under the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001.

Except to the extent provided by subsection (1)﻿(a)﻿(i) and (b)﻿(i), a person does not cease to be entitled to be paid a veteran's pension under that subsection by reason only of changing his or her intentions after leaving New Zealand.
Subsection (3) is for the avoidance of doubt.

prov.heading: Rates of veteran's pension payable overseas

para.text: The rates referred to in section 182(1) are,—
for a single person, a rate that is a proportion (calculated under section 184) of the single sharing accommodation rate:
for a person who is married or in a civil union or in a de facto relationship, a rate that is a proportion (calculated under section 184) of the relationship rate.

If section 171(2) applies to a person for whom an amount is specified by subsection (1), the calculation under section 184 of the proportion of the amount he or she is entitled to be paid must be made in respect of that amount after it has been abated under that section.
This section is subject to section 186.

prov.heading: Calculation of amount of veteran's pension payable overseas

para.text: The proportion referred to in section 183(1) is to be calculated by—
treating each period during which the person concerned has resided in New Zealand while aged 20 or more and less than 65 as whole calendar months and (where applicable) additional days; and
adding the number of additional days (if any), dividing the total by 30, and disregarding any remainder; and
adding the number of calendar months and the quotient calculated under paragraph (b); and
dividing the total calculated under paragraph (c) by 540.

prov.heading: Treatment of certain absences for purposes of calculation

para.text: For the purposes of section 184, a person must be treated as having resided in New Zealand during a period of absence from New Zealand if section 9, 10, or 10A of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001 requires the person to be treated as having been resident and present in New Zealand during the period.

history.history-note: Section 185: replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 32 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

prov.heading: Application for entitlement

para.text: A person is not entitled to be paid a veteran's pension under section 182 unless he or she—
has made an application for the payment of a veteran's pension under that section that states (as the case may be)—
the country or countries in which he or she intends to reside and the period for which he or she intends to reside there; or
the country or countries to and in which he or she intends to travel; and
is ordinarily resident and present in New Zealand on the day he or she makes the application and—
is entitled to a veteran's pension on that day; or
will become entitled to a veteran's pension before he or she leaves New Zealand.

prov.heading: Applications after leaving New Zealand

para.text: This section applies if a person did not make an application for payment under section 182 while ordinarily resident and present in New Zealand because he or she left New Zealand intending to be absent for 26 weeks or less.

VANZ may accept an application for payment in accordance with section 182 if satisfied that the absence for more than 26 weeks is or was due to circumstances beyond the applicant’s control that could not reasonably have been foreseen before leaving New Zealand.

If VANZ has accepted an application under subsection (2) and, after the applicant left New Zealand, the applicant has been paid any instalment of a veteran’s pension in accordance with sections 177 to 180, no liability to repay any of those instalments arises merely because the applicant fails to return to New Zealand within the period allowed for by those sections.

prov.heading: Date of commencement of payment overseas

para.text: The date of commencement of payment overseas of a veteran’s pension under section 182 is the first pay day after the date of the applicant’s departure from New Zealand.
However, in the case of an application under section 187, the date of commencement is the first pay day after the date on which the application was received by VANZ.
Subsection (2) is subject to section 317 of the Social Security Act 2018.

history.history-note: Section 188(3): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: Relationship with other benefit provisions

para.text: A person who is receiving a veteran’s pension overseas under section 182 is not entitled to receive any supplementary or special benefit, temporary additional support, lump sum payment, or any other assistance under the Social Security Act 2018 or under a programme approved under section 100 or 101 of that Act.
When a person who receives a veteran’s pension overseas under section 182 dies, the pension terminates (despite sections 325 to 330 of the Social Security Act 2018) on a date—
to be determined by VANZ; and
that must be not more than 4 weeks after the person’s date of death.
The Social Security Act 2018 applies to veterans’ pensions being paid overseas under section 182, but—
sections 111, 114, 173 to 196, 204, and 206 to 219 of that Act do not apply to those pensions; and
the rest of that Act applies to those pensions except as provided in this section.

history.history-note: Section 189(1): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 189(2): replaced, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 189(3): replaced, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: Specified Pacific country

para.text: In sections 191 to 195, specified Pacific country means a country or territory—
that is listed in Schedule 3; and
that is not a country with which New Zealand has a reciprocity agreement in force under section 380 of the Social Security Act 2018 relating to the portability of the veteran’s pension.
The Governor-General may, by Order in Council, amend Schedule 3 by—
inserting the name of any Pacific country or territory; or
replacing the former name of a country or territory with its new name.
The Order in Council must state the date on which it takes effect, which may be a date before the date on which it is made.
The Order in Council applies to payments of the veteran’s pension under section 191 payable on or after the date on which the order takes effect.
An order under this section—
is secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements); but
commences in accordance with subsection (3), even if it is not yet published.
An order under this section must be confirmed by an Act (see subpart 3 of Part 5 of the Legislation Act 2019).

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and has 3 columns that are grouped under the heading Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section.

entry.emphasis: Legislation Act 2019 requirements for secondary legislation made under this section

row.entry: PCO must publish it on the legislation website and notify it in the Gazette
The Minister must present it to the House of Representatives
It may be disallowed by the House of Representatives

entry.emphasis: This note is not part of the Act.

history.history-note: Section 190(1)﻿(b): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 190(5): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).
Section 190(5): repealed, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 190(6): inserted, on 28 October 2021, by section 3 of the Secondary Legislation Act 2021 (2021 No 7).

prov.label: 190A
prov.heading: Orders are confirmable instruments

para.text: The explanatory note of an Order in Council made under section 190(2) must indicate that—
it is a confirmable instrument, and an annual confirmable instrument, under section 47B of the Legislation Act 2012; and
it is revoked, or taken to have been invalid for past operation, at a time stated in the note, unless earlier confirmed by an Act of Parliament; and
the stated time is the applicable deadline under section 47C(1)﻿(a) or (b) of that Act.

history.history-note: Section 190A: inserted, on 1 January 2016, by section 14 of the Legislation (Confirmable Instruments) Amendment Act 2015 (2015 No 120).

prov.heading: Entitlement to be paid veteran's pension in specified Pacific country

para.text: A person entitled to a veteran’s pension is entitled to be paid the pension in a specified Pacific country at a rate calculated under section 192 if subsection (2) or (3) or (4) applies to the person.
This subsection applies to a person—
who intends to leave New Zealand to reside for more than 52 weeks in a specified Pacific country; and
who is resident and present in New Zealand on the date of his or her application to be paid a veteran’s pension in a specified Pacific country; and
who—
is entitled to a veteran’s pension on the date of the application; or
will be entitled to a veteran’s pension before leaving New Zealand; and
who is resident in a specified Pacific country when each payment of the veteran’s pension is due to be made to him or her.
This subsection applies to a person—

who was, immediately before this Part came into force, being paid a veteran’s pension while resident in a specified Pacific country under section 74O of the War Pensions Act 1954 as it was immediately before that date; and
who is resident in a specified Pacific country when each payment of the veteran’s pension is due to be made to him or her.
This subsection applies to a person—
who, on or after the date this subsection comes into force, makes an application to be paid a veteran’s pension in a specified Pacific country; and
who is resident in the Cook Islands, Niue, or Tokelau on the date of the application; and
who has met the residency requirements for New Zealand superannuation set out in section 8(2) and (3) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001; and
who is resident in a specified Pacific country when each payment of the veteran’s pension is due to be paid to him or her.

If subsection (4) applies, an application to be paid a veteran’s pension in a specified Pacific country must be made in addition to an application to be paid a veteran’s pension under section 297 (and regulations made under section 438) of the Social Security Act 2018.
The applications referred to in subsection (5) may be made together or separately, and may be made—

before an applicant meets the age qualification for New Zealand superannuation in section 7 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001, in anticipation of becoming entitled to be paid a veteran’s pension on reaching the qualifying age; or
when or after the applicant meets the age qualification.

history.history-note: Section 191(1): amended, on 1 July 2015, by section 9(1) of the Social Assistance (Portability to Cook Islands, Niue, and Tokelau) Act 2015 (2015 No 61).
Section 191(4): inserted, on 1 July 2015, by section 9(2) of the Social Assistance (Portability to Cook Islands, Niue, and Tokelau) Act 2015 (2015 No 61).
Section 191(4)﻿(c): replaced, on 16 November 2021, by section 12 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income (Fair Residency) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 46).
Section 191(5): inserted, on 1 July 2015, by section 9(2) of the Social Assistance (Portability to Cook Islands, Niue, and Tokelau) Act 2015 (2015 No 61).
Section 191(5): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 191(6): inserted, on 1 July 2015, by section 9(2) of the Social Assistance (Portability to Cook Islands, Niue, and Tokelau) Act 2015 (2015 No 61).

prov.heading: Rates of payment of veteran's pension payable to people resident in specified Pacific countries

para.text: The rate of veteran’s pension payable under section 191 is,—
in the case of a person who has resided in New Zealand for 20 or more years since turning 20 years, the base rate:
in the case of a person who has resided in New Zealand for 10 or more years but fewer than 20 years since turning 20 years, an amount calculated using the following formula:
where—
is the base rate
is the whole number of years the person has resided in New Zealand since turning 20 years:

in the case of a person who is resident in a specified Pacific country and was, immediately before section 74P(1)﻿(c) of the War Pensions Act 1954 came into force, being paid a veteran’s pension under section 17BA of the Social Welfare (Reciprocity Agreements, and New Zealand Artificial Limb Service) Act 1990 as it was immediately before that date, the higher of—
the amount he or she was being paid immediately before that date (subject to abatement under section 171(2) if that section applies to the person); and
the appropriate amount payable under paragraph (a) or (b).
For the purposes of this section, the base rate is,—
in the case of a single person, the single sharing accommodation rate (subject to abatement under section 171 if that section applies to the person); and
in the case of a person who is married or in a civil union or in a de facto relationship, the relationship rate (subject to abatement under section 171(2) if that section applies to the person).
However, if sections 187 to 191 of the Social Security Act 2018 applies, that amount must be reduced by the amount deducted in accordance with that section.

history.history-note: Section 192(3): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: Commencement and termination of payments

para.text: The commencement date of payment of a pension paid under section 191 is,—
in the case of a person to whom section 191(2) applies, the first pay day after the date of the person’s departure from New Zealand; and
in the case of a person to whom section 191(3) applies, the first pay day after the date on which this section comes into force; and
in the case of a person to whom section 191(4) applies, the later of—
the date that the person meets the age qualification for New Zealand superannuation in section 7 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001; and
the date that the application referred to in section 191(4) is made.
When a person being paid a pension under section 191 dies, the pension terminates on a date to be determined by VANZ, being a date not more than 4 weeks after the person's date of death.

history.history-note: Section 193(1)﻿(b): amended, on 1 July 2015, by section 10(1) of the Social Assistance (Portability to Cook Islands, Niue, and Tokelau) Act 2015 (2015 No 61).
Section 193(1)﻿(c): inserted, on 1 July 2015, by section 10(2) of the Social Assistance (Portability to Cook Islands, Niue, and Tokelau) Act 2015 (2015 No 61).

prov.heading: Effect on other benefits

para.text: A person who is receiving a veteran's pension overseas under section 191 is not entitled to receive any supplementary or special benefit, temporary additional support, lump sum payment, or any other assistance under the Social Security Act 2018 or under a programme approved under section 100 or 101 of that Act.

history.history-note: Section 194: amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: Application of this Act and Social Security Act 2018

para.text: Sections 176 to 189 do not apply in respect of a veteran’s pension being paid in a specified Pacific country under section 191.
The Social Security Act 2018 (other than sections 204, 206 to 208, 217 to 219, 311, and 313 to 315) applies to a veteran’s pension being paid in a specified Pacific country under section 191.

history.history-note: Section 195 heading: amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 195(2): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: Certain former economic pensioners may be paid up to the full rate of veteran's pension overseas

para.text: If, immediately before 1 April 1990, a person residing outside New Zealand was being paid an economic pension under the War Pensions Act 1954 as it was immediately before that date, VANZ may pay the full rate, or any lesser percentage of the full rate (being not less than 50%), of a veteran’s pension to that person while he or she remains a resident outside New Zealand.
This section applies despite—
sections 181 to 189; and
sections 204 and 219 of the Social Security Act 2018.

history.history-note: Section 196(1): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).
Section 196(2): inserted, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

subpart.heading: Establishment and functions of VANZ and related matters

prov.heading: Chief of Defence Force responsible for administration of Act

para.text: The Chief of Defence Force is responsible to the Minister for the efficient, effective, and economic administration of this Act.

prov.heading: Establishment of VANZ as unit within Defence Force

para.text: The Chief of Defence Force must establish a unit within the Defence Force to be called Veterans' Affairs New Zealand.

prov.heading: Functions of VANZ

para.text: The functions of VANZ are—
to provide entitlements to veterans and other claimants in accordance with this Act; and
to arrange for the provision of treatment, rehabilitation, and related services to veterans and other claimants in accordance with this Act; and
to ensure that systems and processes are in place to enable VANZ, the Defence Force, and the Accident Compensation Corporation to exercise their respective responsibilities under section 87; and
to supply, or to contribute to the cost of, plaques and headstones for deceased service and ex-service persons and their spouses and partners in accordance with this Act; and
to contribute to the maintenance and development of cemeteries or parts of cemeteries set aside under section 15 of the Burial and Cremation Act 1964; and
to administer the Veterans' Medical Research Trust Fund; and
to provide advice to the Minister on any matters relating to veterans; and
to undertake any other functions relating to veterans and their families as directed to be undertaken by the Minister or Chief of Defence Force; and
to undertake any functions related to or incidental to the functions specified in paragraphs (a) to (h).

prov.heading: General Manager and Deputy General Manager of VANZ

para.text: The Chief of Defence Force must appoint suitable persons to be the General Manager of VANZ, and Deputy General Manager of VANZ.
The general manager has and may perform and exercise, subject to the control and direction of the Chief of Defence Force, all the functions and powers necessary for the administration of this Act.

The deputy general manager has and may perform and exercise, subject to the control and direction of the Chief of Defence Force or the general manager, all the functions and powers necessary for the administration of this Act.

If there is a vacancy in the office of the general manager, or if the general manager is absent from duty for any reason, the deputy general manager has and may perform and exercise all the functions, duties, and powers of the general manager for as long as the vacancy or absence continues.

The fact that the deputy general manager performs or exercises any function, duty, or power of the general manager is, in the absence of proof to the contrary, conclusive evidence of the deputy general manager's authority to do so.

prov.heading: Functions, duties, and powers of VANZ

para.text: Every function, duty, and power of VANZ under this Act is a function, duty, or power of the general manager.

prov.heading: General manager may delegate functions, duties, and powers to members of Defence Force

para.text: The general manager may delegate, either generally or particularly, any of his or her functions, duties, and powers under this Act to any member of the Defence Force.
A delegation may be made under this section to—
a specified person; or
a person belonging to a specified class of persons; or
the holder of a specified office or appointment in the Defence Force; or
the holder of an office or appointment of a specified class in the Defence Force.
The delegation—
must be written; and
may include a power to subdelegate any function, duty, or power to any other member of the Defence Force; and
may be made subject to any restrictions and conditions that the general manager thinks fit; and
is revocable at any time, in writing; and
does not prevent the performance of a function or duty, or the exercise of a power, by the general manager.

Subject to any restrictions or conditions imposed by the general manager, a person to whom any functions, duties, or powers are delegated may perform and exercise them in the same manner, subject to the same restrictions, and with the same effect as if they had been conferred directly by this Act and not by delegation.
A person who appears to act under a delegation is presumed to be acting in accordance with its terms in the absence of evidence to the contrary.

prov.heading: General manager may delegate functions, duties, and powers outside Defence Force with approval

para.text: The general manager may delegate any of his or her functions, duties, and powers under this Act,—
with the written approval of the Chief of Defence Force, to a person described in clause 2(4) of Schedule 6 of the Public Service Act 2020:
with the written approval of the Chief of Defence Force and the Minister, to a person described in clause 2(5) of Schedule 6 of the Public Service Act 2020.

Clauses 2 to 4 of Schedule 6 of the Public Service Act 2020 apply, with any necessary modifications, to a delegation under subsection (1) as if the general manager were a public service chief executive.
This section does not limit section 160.

history.history-note: Section 203(1)﻿(a): amended, on 7 August 2020, by section 135 of the Public Service Act 2020 (2020 No 40).
Section 203(1)﻿(b): amended, on 7 August 2020, by section 135 of the Public Service Act 2020 (2020 No 40).
Section 203(2): replaced, on 7 August 2020, by section 135 of the Public Service Act 2020 (2020 No 40).

prov.heading: Immunity from civil liability

para.text: This section applies to—
the general manager:
the deputy general manager:
any member of the Defence Force performing or exercising a function, duty, or power under this Act:
a review officer:
the RSA member of the review panel:
a member of the appeal board:
a member of the advisory board:
a member of the advisory panel.
The person is immune from liability in civil proceedings for good-faith actions or omissions in pursuance or intended pursuance of their functions, duties, or powers.

Despite subsection (2), to the extent that the person's actions or omissions are not done or omitted in the discharge or purported discharge of the person's responsibilities of a judicial nature or in connection with the execution of judicial process,—
a court may find the Crown itself liable in tort in respect of the actions or omissions; and
for the purpose of determining whether the Crown is so liable, the court must disregard the immunity in subsection (2).

prov.heading: VANZ may reconsider decision

para.text: If VANZ considers it made a decision in error, it may reconsider the decision at any time, whatever the reason for the error.
This section applies to a decision by VANZ that affects a person and in respect of which—
an application for review of the decision did not succeed (in whole or in part); and
an appeal against the unsuccessful review decision did not succeed (in whole or in part).
Without limiting subsection (1), VANZ may reconsider a decision if, in VANZ's view, any of the following apply:

a statement of principles is adopted or modified under section 22 after the decision was made and, if the statement had been adopted or modified before the decision was made, it would have materially affected the decision; or

a presumptive decision-making condition is specified or amended in regulations made after the decision was made and, if the condition had been specified or amended before the decision was made, it would have materially affected the decision; or

service is declared operational service under section 9 after the decision was made and, if the declaration had been made before the decision was made, it would have materially affected the decision; or
VANZ receives new information and, if that information had been received before the decision was made, it would have materially affected the decision.
VANZ may reconsider a decision to which subsection (3)﻿(a) or (b) applies on application by the affected person or on its own initiative.
VANZ may reconsider a decision to which subsection (3)﻿(c) applies on application by the affected person.
A decision by VANZ to decline to reconsider a decision under this section is not subject to review under section 215.
If VANZ decides to reconsider a decision under this section, VANZ must provide the claimant with an opportunity to make a submission on the decision before it is reconsidered.

If, following reconsideration, VANZ decides that a person has an entitlement, the entitlement commences on the date it would have commenced on had VANZ decided that the person had the entitlement at the outset.
Any decision made as a consequence of the reconsideration of a decision under this section is subject to review under section 215.

history.history-note: Section 205(2): amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 30(1) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 205(3): amended, on 1 October 2020, by section 30(2) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).
Section 205(3)﻿(d): inserted, on 1 October 2020, by section 30(3) of the Veterans’ Support Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 54).

prov.heading: Recovery of debts by VANZ

para.text: An overpayment by VANZ is a debt due to VANZ if the overpayment is—
an amount paid to a person that exceeds the amount to which the person is entitled under this Act or that was payable under the War Pensions Act 1954; or
an amount paid to a person who has no entitlement to it under this Act or the War Pensions Act 1954.
VANZ may—
recover the debt by way of proceedings; or
deduct all or part of the debt from any amount payable to that person by VANZ.
However, if a person receives a payment from VANZ in good faith, VANZ may not—
recover all or any part of the payment on the ground only that the decision under which the payment was made has been revised on medical grounds; or
recover all or any part of the payment if the recipient—
did not intentionally contribute to the error; and
has so altered his or her position in reliance on the validity of the payment that it would be inequitable to require repayment.

prov.heading: Entitlements inalienable

para.text: All entitlements are absolutely inalienable, whether by way of, or in accordance with, a sale, assignment, charge, execution, bankruptcy, or otherwise.
This section does not affect—
any right of VANZ to recover any amounts under this Act or to make any deductions authorised by this Act from any entitlements that VANZ is liable to provide; or
sections 4 and 5 of the Maori Housing Act 1935; or
sections 154 to 161 of the District Court Act 2016; or
sections 87(2)﻿(b), 88AE(1)﻿(a), 103, 104, and 105 to 106B of the Summary Proceedings Act 1957; or
section 27Y of the Social Security Act 1964 (as saved by section 256(1) of the Child Support Act 1991) or regulations made under section 444 of the Social Security Act 2018; or
sections 105, 110, 118, and 121 of the Family Proceedings Act 1980 (as saved by section 259(1) of the Child Support Act 1991); or
the Child Support Act 1991; or
subparts 1, 2, and 5 of Part 2 or section 193 of the Student Loan Scheme Act 2011; or
subpart 1 of Part 3 of the KiwiSaver Act 2006; or
section 157 of the Tax Administration Act 1994; or
section 41B of the Legal Services Act 2011.

history.history-note: Section 207(2)﻿(c): replaced, on 1 March 2017, by section 261 of the District Court Act 2016 (2016 No 49).
Section 207(2)﻿(e): amended, on 26 November 2018, by section 459 of the Social Security Act 2018 (2018 No 32).

prov.heading: Entitlements to be provided to claimant only

para.text: VANZ must provide entitlements only to the claimant to whom VANZ is liable to provide the entitlements.
Subsection (1) does not apply—
to payments made by VANZ directly to a person for providing entitlements to the claimant; or
to payments authorised by regulations made under section 265 to be made to any other person; or
to payments made under section 209 or 210.

prov.heading: VANZ to pay amount for child to caregiver or financially responsible person

para.text: This section applies if an entitlement provided to a claimant who is not yet 16 years old is solely a payment of money.
VANZ must make the payment—
to a person who is caring for the claimant; or

if VANZ considers that it would not be appropriate to make the payment to such a person, to another person or to trustees who, in either case, VANZ considers will apply the payment as required by subsection (3).
A person to whom a payment is made under subsection (2) must apply it for the maintenance, education, advancement, or benefit of the claimant.
VANZ is not under an obligation to see to the application of any money paid under this section, and is not liable to the claimant in respect of any such payment.

prov.heading: VANZ to pay amount to claimant's estate

para.text: Subsection (2) applies to any entitlement (other than a lump sum payment) that is a payment that VANZ—
is liable to make to a claimant, but that has accrued and is unpaid at the date of the claimant's death; or
would have been liable to make to the claimant, if the claimant had made a claim for it.
VANZ is liable to pay the amount to the claimant's estate if the estate applies for it within 3 years after the date of the claimant's death.

prov.heading: Defence Force to report on timeliness of decision-making and implementation of systems and procedures

para.text: The Defence Force must, in its annual report,—
provide a comparison of—
the period of time on average that VANZ takes to make decisions on claims for entitlement under this Act during the year; and
the 30-working-day period specified under section 11(2) (or any shorter period agreed between the Minister and VANZ); and
report on the implementation of the systems and procedures referred to in section 199(c).

prov.heading: Appointment of assessors and examiners

para.text: VANZ must appoint as many health practitioners as it considers necessary to do assessments or examinations under sections 27(1)﻿(c) and (2)﻿(c), 124, 130, and 166(1), and clause 48 or 50 of Schedule 2.
VANZ may appoint a health practitioner to do assessments under the Act generally, or in respect of particular sections of the Act.
VANZ must pay assessors to do assessments and, if fees and allowances of assessors are prescribed in or under regulations made under section 265, VANZ must pay prescribed amounts.

In appointing a person to be an assessor, VANZ must have regard to the skills, qualifications, and training that VANZ considers appropriate for a person to do the assessments for which it is proposed to appoint the person an assessor.
VANZ may appoint a medical practitioner to do assessments under section 134 only if the medical practitioner meets the requirements described in subsection (6) or (7).
A medical practitioner who provides general medical services must also—
have an interest, and proven work experience, in disability management in the workplace or in occupational rehabilitation; and
have at least 5 years' experience in general practice; and
meet at least 1 of the following criteria:
be a Fellow of the Royal New Zealand College of General Practitioners or hold an equivalent qualification:
be undertaking training towards becoming a Fellow of the Royal New Zealand College of General Practitioners or holding an equivalent qualification:
have undertaken relevant advanced training.
A medical practitioner who does not provide general medical services must—
have an interest, and proven work experience, in disability management in the workplace or in occupational rehabilitation; and
be a member of a recognised college.

prov.heading: Purposes for which VANZ may collect information

para.text: VANZ may collect information for the following purposes:
to enable a comprehensive claims database to be maintained:
to facilitate the monitoring of the operation of this Act:
to monitor and evaluate the nature, incidence, severity, and consequences of service-related illnesses and injuries:
the provision of appropriate rehabilitation and treatment:
the provision of appropriate compensation:
policy development under this Act.

prov.heading: Effect of review or appeal on decisions

para.text: A decision by VANZ that relates to a veteran's or other claimant's entitlement continues to be of full effect even though—
an applicant has made a review application relating to the decision; or
any other proceeding relating to the decision has been commenced.
A review decision continues to be of full effect, unless subsection (3) applies, even though—
an appellant has filed a notice of appeal relating to the review decision; or
any other proceeding relating to the review decision has been commenced.
A review decision ceases to be of full effect if all the parties to the review agree to a variation of it for the benefit of the claimant.
A review decision is subject to section 237 (the appeal board's powers to determine an appeal).

prov.heading: Review of decision

para.text: A veteran or other claimant may apply for a review of a decision by VANZ that relates to that person's entitlement, including—
a decision of VANZ made under Part 3, 4, or 5 if the decision relates to 1 or more of the following:
eligibility for an entitlement:
whether there is a relationship between a veteran's injury, illness, or death and the veteran's qualifying service:
whether and, if so, to what extent a veteran's service is qualifying service:
the degree of impairment caused by an injury or illness:
a decision of VANZ to decline entitlement to a veteran's pension under Part 6 on the basis that the service on which a claim for entitlement is based is not qualifying operational service.
Subsection (1) does not limit or affect any other right or appeal provided for under this Act.

prov.heading: Procedure for reviews

para.text: The procedure for reviews may be prescribed in regulations made under section 265.
Regulations referred to in subsection (1) prescribing the procedure for reviews—
may prescribe different procedures to apply in respect of different types of review; and
must not be inconsistent with the provisions of this subpart.

prov.heading: Application for review

para.text: A review application is made by giving an application that complies with subsection (2) to VANZ.
The application must—
be in writing:
be made in the form provided by VANZ for the purpose:
identify the decision or decisions in respect of which it is made:
state the grounds on which it is made.
An applicant may also provide written submissions and any information (whether prepared by the applicant or any other person) in support of the application.

prov.heading: Time limit for application for review

para.text: The applicant must apply for review within 6 months after receiving notification of the decision.
However, a review application may be made later if the review officer is satisfied that the delay was caused by circumstances beyond the applicant's control.

prov.heading: Review to be conducted by review officer or review panel

para.text: A review of the decision must be conducted by a review officer unless subsection (3) applies.
If an application for review relates to—
a decision made by the deputy general manager, the review must be conducted by a review officer appointed by the general manager:
a decision made by the general manager, the review must be conducted by a review officer appointed by the Chief of Defence Force.
This subsection applies to a review of a decision of VANZ—
to decline entitlement under this Act on the basis that the service on which the claim for entitlement is based is not qualifying service; or
to decline entitlement to a veteran's pension under Part 6 on the basis that the service on which a claim for entitlement is based is not qualifying operational service.
If subsection (3) applies—
the review must be conducted by the review panel instead of a review officer; and
this subpart applies, with all necessary modifications, to a review by the review panel as if the review were conducted by a review officer.

prov.heading: Conduct of review

para.text: A review officer must act independently when conducting a review.
A review officer must not conduct a review if the review officer has had any previous involvement with the claim other than as a review officer.
The review officer must—
decide the review on the basis of its substantive merits under this Act:
conduct the review on the papers without a hearing:
comply with—
the principles of natural justice; and
the principles specified in section 10(b); and
any other relevant provision of this Act and any regulations made under this Act in accordance with section 216.

prov.heading: Review officer may require further information or assessment

para.text: The review officer may require the applicant or VANZ to provide any information that the review officer considers is reasonably necessary to decide the review.
The review officer may seek an assessment of the person's impairment only if the review officer considers that the assessment is reasonably necessary to decide the review.
The medical assessment must be conducted in accordance with any regulations made under section 265 for the conduct of medical assessments.

prov.heading: Decision of review officer

para.text: The review officer must—
confirm the decision; or
modify the decision; or
revoke the decision; or
make any other decision that is appropriate to the circumstances of the case.
If the review officer revokes the decision, the review officer must—
substitute the review officer's decision for that of VANZ; or
require VANZ to make the decision again in accordance with directions that the review officer gives to VANZ.

prov.heading: Veterans' Service Review Panel

para.text: The general manager may appoint a Veterans' Service Review Panel (the review panel)—
from time to time; or
in respect of a particular review.
The review panel has the function specified in subsection (3) in relation to a review of a decision of VANZ—
to decline entitlement under this Act on the basis that the service on which the claim for entitlement is based is not qualifying service; or
to decline entitlement to a veteran's pension under Part 6 on the basis that the service on which a claim for entitlement is based is not qualifying operational service.
The function of the review panel is to conduct the review in accordance with section 219(4).

prov.heading: Membership of review panel

para.text: The general manager must only appoint members who, in the general manager's view, have qualifications and experience that are relevant to the review panel's function.
The review panel must comprise—
an employee or contractor of the New Zealand Defence Force; and
a person nominated by the Royal New Zealand Returned and Services' Association (the RSA member).

prov.heading: Review panel may seek and receive further information

para.text: The review panel may seek and receive any information that the review panel considers reasonably necessary to decide the review from any person with relevant expertise (for example, an historian).
The review panel's ability to seek and receive information under this section is in addition to the ability to require information under section 221(1).

prov.heading: Decision stands if panel unable to agree

para.text: If the members of the review panel are unable to agree how to decide a review, the decision under review is to be treated as if it has been confirmed by the review panel.

prov.heading: RSA member

para.text: The RSA member of the review panel is entitled, in accordance with the fees framework,—
to receive remuneration for services as a member at a rate and of a kind determined by the Minister; and
to be reimbursed for actual and reasonable travelling and other expenses incurred in carrying out his or her office as a member.
For the purposes of subsection (1), fees framework has the same meaning as in section 10 of the Crown Entities Act 2004.
The RSA member may resign at any time by giving written notice to the general manager.

prov.heading: Appeal against review decision

para.text: A review decision made under this subpart may be appealed by the person who applied for the review or by VANZ.
An appeal is brought by the appellant giving a notice of appeal to the other party.
The notice of appeal must be in writing and made in the form provided for the purpose by VANZ.
An appeal must be brought within 6 months after the date of the review decision.
However, the appeal board may, in its discretion, extend the time for bringing an appeal if the appeal board thinks an extension is in the interests of justice.
As soon as practicable after an appeal is brought, VANZ must provide to the appeal board—
the notice of appeal; and
the review decision under appeal; and
any application, written submissions, statements, reports, and other documents held by VANZ that relate to the review decision.
VANZ must, at the time that it provides the documents specified in subsection (6)﻿(c) to the appeal board, provide a copy of the documents to the other party.

prov.heading: Hearing and determination of appeal

para.text: An appeal made to the appeal board is a de novo appeal, and the appeal board is not bound by any findings of fact made by the decision maker whose decision is the subject of the appeal.
Appeals must, in accordance with section 10(b)﻿(iv), be heard and determined without regard to legal or procedural technicalities.
The appeal board may, for the purposes of hearing an appeal,—
receive any evidence or information that, in its opinion, may assist it to determine the appeal, whether or not that evidence or information would be admissible in a court of law:
take evidence on oath or affirmation, and for that purpose an oath or affirmation may be administered by the chairperson of the appeal board:

permit a witness to give evidence by any means, including by written or electronic means or in person or remotely, and, if the appeal board considers it appropriate, require the witness to verify the evidence by oath or affirmation.
The appeal board may, on application or on its own initiative, hear an appeal or part of an appeal in private.
The appeal board must comply with—
the principles of natural justice; and
the principles specified in section 10(b); and
any other relevant provision of this Act and any regulations made under section 265 of this Act.

prov.heading: Composition of appeal board

para.text: Every appeal must be heard by not fewer than 3 members—
1 of whom must be a medical practitioner; and
1 of whom must be the chairperson.
Every appeal must be determined by a majority of the votes of the members hearing the appeal, and the chairperson has a casting vote if voting is equal.

prov.heading: Power to require documents

para.text: The appeal board may, for the purposes of hearing an appeal, require any person to—
produce any documents or things in that person's possession or control or copies of those documents or things:
allow copies or representations of those documents or things to be made:
verify by statutory declaration any written information, copies of documents, or representations of things provided to the inquiry.

prov.heading: Power to summon witnesses

para.text: The appeal board may issue a witness summons in writing to any person, requiring that person to attend and give evidence at a hearing before the appeal board.
The witness summons must state—
the place where, and the date and time when, the person is to attend; and
the documents or things in that person's possession or control that he or she is required to produce to the appeal board; and
the person's entitlement to be paid costs and travelling expenses, in accordance with section 235; and
the penalty for failing to attend.

prov.heading: Service of summons on witness

para.text: Unless a witness has consented to service by another means, a summons must be served personally on that witness by delivering a sealed copy of the summons to the witness not later than 24 hours before the witness must attend the hearing.
Despite subsection (1), the appeal board may direct substituted service in accordance with the High Court Rules 2016.

history.history-note: Section 233(2): amended, on 18 October 2016, by section 183(c) of the Senior Courts Act 2016 (2016 No 48).

prov.heading: Offence to fail to comply with summons

para.text: Any person who intentionally, and without reasonable excuse, fails to attend a hearing in accordance with the notice of summons commits an offence and is liable, on conviction, to a fine not exceeding $2,000.

prov.heading: Reimbursement of costs and travelling expenses of appellant, witnesses, and other participants

para.text: The following are entitled to be paid for their reasonable costs and travelling expenses to attend the hearing of an appeal:
the appellant; and
any person summoned to attend a hearing.
The appeal board may also order that other persons who participate in the hearing of an appeal be paid for their reasonable costs and travelling expenses.

The level of reimbursement payable for reasonable costs and travelling expenses is the level prescribed by regulations made under section 265 or, in the absence of regulations, determined by the appeal board.

prov.heading: Immunities and privileges of participants

para.text: Witnesses and other persons participating in the hearing of an appeal under this subpart (other than counsel) have the same immunities and privileges as if they were appearing in civil proceedings and the provisions of subpart 8 of Part 2 of the Evidence Act 2006 apply to the appeal, to the extent that they are relevant, as if—
the appeal were a civil proceeding; and
every reference to a Judge were a reference to the appeal board.
Counsel appearing before the appeal board have the same immunities and privileges as they would have if appearing before a court.

prov.heading: Decision of appeal board

para.text: The appeal board must—
confirm the review decision; or
modify the review decision; or
revoke the review decision; or
make any other decision that is appropriate to the circumstances of the case.
If the appeal board revokes the review decision, the appeal board must—
substitute the appeal board's decision for that of the review officer or the review panel (as the case may be); or
require VANZ to make the decision again in accordance with directions that the appeal board gives to VANZ.

prov.heading: Decision to be made available unless publication prohibited or restricted

para.text: A decision of the appeal board must be in writing and be signed by the members of the board who heard the appeal.
Subject to subsection (3), VANZ must make decisions of the appeal board available on its Internet site.
The appeal board may, on application by the appellant or on its own initiative, make an order prohibiting or restricting the publication of—
any report of a hearing:
the decision or any part of a decision:
the name of any person and any details of the decision that would identify the person.
Any person who contravenes an order made under subsection (3) commits an offence and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $5,000.

prov.heading: Appeal to High Court on questions of law

para.text: An appeal against a determination of the appeal board may be made to the High Court, in accordance with the rules of court, only on a question of law.

prov.heading: Veterans' Entitlements Appeal Board

para.text: This section establishes the Veterans' Entitlements Appeal Board (the appeal board).
The function of the appeal board is to determine appeals against review decisions.
The appeal board may, subject to any provision in this Act or regulations made under this Act, regulate its own procedure.

prov.heading: Membership of appeal board

para.text: The appeal board consists of not more than 4 members of which—
1 must be a lawyer (within the meaning of the Lawyers and Conveyancers Act 2006) who has at least 7 years' standing; and
2 must be medical practitioners, 1 of whom must be nominated by the Royal New Zealand Returned and Services' Association.
The Minister must appoint members of the appeal board on any terms and conditions that the Minister thinks fit.
The Minister must appoint the member referred to in subsection (1)﻿(a) as the chairperson.

prov.heading: Deputy members

para.text: For each member that the Minister appoints, the Minister may appoint a deputy member to act in the place of the member if the member is not available to attend and deliberate on an appeal.

Every deputy member of the appeal board who acts in the place of a member of the appeal board has the powers and capacity of the member, and the fact that any deputy acts in the place of a member is proof of the deputy’s authority to do so.

prov.heading: Specialist member

para.text: The chairperson may appoint a specialist member to assist the members of the appeal board hearing an appeal if—
the appeal involves technical, scientific, or medical matters; and
the appointment of a specialist member would substantially assist the members of the appeal board to determine the appeal.

A specialist member may participate in the hearing of the appeal to the same extent as the other members hearing the appeal, except that the specialist member is not a member of the appeal board for the purposes of making a determination.
A specialist member is appointed only for the purposes of a particular appeal and ceases to hold office when the members of the appeal board have determined the appeal.

prov.heading: Appeal board serviced by VANZ

para.text: VANZ must provide the resources and administrative support necessary to enable the appeal board to efficiently and effectively perform its functions.

prov.heading: Term of appointment

para.text: The term of office of a member of the appeal board must not exceed 3 years.
A member of the appeal board may be reappointed.
A member may resign at any time by giving written notice to the Minister.
The Minister may at any time remove a member of the appeal board for inability to perform the functions of a member of the appeal board, bankruptcy, neglect of duty, or misconduct.

prov.heading: Remuneration of members of appeal board

para.text: Each member of the appeal board is entitled, in accordance with the fees framework,—
to receive remuneration for services as a member at a rate and of a kind determined by the Minister; and
to be reimbursed for actual and reasonable travelling and other expenses incurred in carrying out his or her office as a member.
For the purposes of subsection (1), fees framework has the same meaning as in section 10 of the Crown Entities Act 2004.

part.heading: Advisory bodies and medical research trust fund

prov.heading: Veterans' Advisory Board

para.text: This section establishes the Veterans' Advisory Board (the advisory board).
The function of the advisory board is to provide advice to the Minister on its own motion or on request, including advice on policies to be applied in respect of veterans’ entitlement.
The Minister may give terms of reference on the advice that the advisory board provides to the Minister.
The advisory board may, subject to any provision of this Act or regulations made under this Act, determine its own procedure.

prov.heading: Membership of advisory board

para.text: The advisory board consists of not more than 7 members and 1 serving veteran, nominated by the Chief of Defence Force, as an ex officio member.
The Minister must appoint members of the advisory board on any terms and conditions that the Minister thinks fit.
The Minister must appoint a chairperson and a deputy chairperson of the advisory board.
If the chairperson and the deputy chairperson are both absent from any meeting, the remaining members may appoint one of their number to act as chairperson at that meeting.
The Minister must endeavour to appoint members who are representative of the veteran community.
The general manager and any veterans group may nominate a person for appointment as a member and the Minister must have regard to, but is not bound by, the nomination.
The Minister may, by notice in writing to a member, remove the member from office for misconduct, inability to perform the functions of office, or neglect of duty.

prov.heading: Deputy members

para.text: For each member that the Minister appoints, the Minister may appoint a deputy member to act in the place of the member for whom he or she is deputy while the member is unable to attend any meeting of the advisory board.

Every deputy member of the advisory board, while so acting, will have the powers and capacity of a member of the advisory board, and the fact that any deputy attends a meeting is proof of the deputy’s authority to do so.

prov.heading: Meetings of advisory board

para.text: Meetings of the advisory board are to be held at the times and places that the advisory board or the chairperson may from time to time determine.
The quorum for the advisory board is 4 members.

prov.heading: Advisory board serviced by VANZ

para.text: VANZ must provide the resources and administrative support necessary to enable the advisory board to perform its functions.

prov.heading: Term of appointment

para.text: The term of office of a member of the advisory board must not exceed 3 years.
A member of the advisory board may be reappointed.
A member may resign at any time by written notice to the Minister.

prov.heading: Remuneration of members of advisory board

para.text: Each member of the advisory board, other than the ex officio member, is entitled, in accordance with the fees framework,—
to receive remuneration for services as a member at a rate and of a kind determined by the Minister; and
to be reimbursed for actual and reasonable travelling and other expenses incurred in carrying out his or her office as a member.
For the purposes of subsection (1), fees framework has the same meaning as in section 10 of the Crown Entities Act 2004.

prov.heading: Veterans' Health Advisory Panel

para.text: This section establishes the Veterans' Health Advisory Panel (the advisory panel).
The functions of the advisory panel are—
to provide advice and guidelines to the Minister on—
assessing the relationship between qualifying operational service and impairment for the purpose of assessing eligibility to entitlements:
the impacts of service on veterans’ health:
monitoring veterans' health:
injuries or illnesses for which it should be presumed that deterioration after a veteran has left service is not service-related:
the assessment and determination of claims by VANZ and medical assessors:
to provide comments to the Minister on reports prepared by VANZ and provided to it under section 22, 23, or 24:
to provide advice to the Minister on the pairing of organs under section 20 and on conditions to be treated as linked to service under section 21:
to decide how the income of the Veterans' Medical Research Trust Fund is to be applied for grants and awards:
to carry out any other functions the Minister requires that are consistent with this Act and any other enactment.
The advisory panel may, subject to any provision in this Act or regulations made under this Act, regulate its own procedure.

prov.heading: Membership of advisory panel

para.text: The advisory panel consists of not more than 10 members, of whom the following are ex officio members:
a medical practitioner nominated by VANZ:
a medical practitioner nominated by the Chief of Defence Force:
a person nominated by the advisory board as its representative.
A member appointed under subsection (1)﻿(a), (b), or (c) may attend and speak at meetings of the advisory panel but may not vote on any matter before the panel.
The Minister must appoint suitably qualified persons as members of the advisory panel on any terms and conditions that the Minister thinks fit.
The Minister must appoint a chairperson and a deputy chairperson.
If the chairperson and the deputy chairperson are both absent from any meeting, the remaining members may appoint one of their number to act as chairperson at that meeting.
The Minister may, by notice in writing to a member, remove the member from office for misconduct, inability to perform the functions of office, or neglect of duty.

prov.heading: Advisory panel serviced by VANZ

para.text: VANZ must provide the resources and administrative support necessary to enable the advisory panel to perform its functions.

prov.heading: Meetings of advisory panel

para.text: Meetings of the advisory panel must be held at the times and places that the advisory panel or the chairperson may from time to time appoint.
The quorum for the advisory panel is 5 members.

Every decision on how to apply the income of the Veterans' Medical Research Trust Fund must be made at a meeting of the advisory panel and by a majority of the votes of the members present and voting on it.

When the advisory panel makes a decision referred to in subsection (3), the person for the time being acting as the chairperson has a deliberative vote and, if the voting is equal, has a casting vote.

prov.heading: Term of appointment

para.text: The term of office of a member of the advisory panel other than an ex officio member must not exceed 3 years.
A member may be reappointed.
A member may resign at any time by written notice to the Minister.

prov.heading: Remuneration of members of advisory panel

para.text: Every member of the advisory panel, other than the ex officio members, is entitled, in accordance with the fees framework,—
to receive remuneration for services as a member at a rate and of a kind determined by the Minister; and
to be reimbursed for actual and reasonable travelling and other expenses incurred in carrying out his or her office as a member.
For the purposes of subsection (1), fees framework has the same meaning as in section 10 of the Crown Entities Act 2004.

prov.heading: Veterans’ Medical Research Trust Fund

para.text: The fund existing under the name of the War Pensions Medical Research Trust Fund immediately before the commencement of this Part continues under the name Veterans' Medical Research Trust Fund (the fund).
VANZ administers the fund in accordance with this Act.
The capital of the fund comprises—
all property held by or on behalf of the War Pensions Medical Research Trust Fund on the commencement of this Act:
all property held by or on behalf of the War Pensions Advisory Board immediately before the commencement of this Act:
all money paid into the fund by way of grants, donations, or gifts:
all property held by the Crown as capital of the fund:
all money derived from the sale of any property held by the Crown as capital of the fund:
all income transferred to capital under section 261(3).
The income of the fund comprises—
all income derived from the investment of money by VANZ under section 263:
all income derived from the administration of property held by the Crown as capital of the fund:
all money derived from the leasing of land:
all other money not forming part of the capital of the fund.

prov.heading: Income to be applied for grants and awards

para.text: VANZ must apply the income of the fund by making grants or awards for the purpose of research into any field of medicine that the advisory panel considers may be beneficial for veterans.
VANZ must meet the costs incurred in the administration of the fund from the income.
VANZ may transfer any income of the fund that is not required for expenditure under subsections (1) and (2) to the capital of the fund.
In making decisions in respect of grants or awards under subsection (1), the advisory panel must—
act fairly and transparently; and
implement systems and procedures to enable it to give effect to the principle set out in paragraph (a); and
make information about those systems and procedures available on VANZ's Internet site.

prov.heading: Annual report and audit of fund

para.text: VANZ must prepare an annual report of the fund, including—
annual financial statements prepared in accordance with generally accepted accounting practice (within the meaning of section 2(1) of the Public Finance Act 1989); and
a report on the fund's implementation of the systems and procedures referred to in section 261(4)﻿(b).
The fund's annual report must include a statement of responsibility in respect of the fund's financial statements and the report on the fund's implementation of the systems and procedures.
Section 45C of the Public Finance Act 1989 applies to the statement of responsibility as if VANZ were a department and the general manager were the department's chief executive.
The fund is to be treated as a public entity as defined in section 4 of the Public Audit Act 2001 and, in accordance with that Act, the Auditor-General is its auditor.
The Defence Force must ensure that the fund’s annual report is included in the annual report prepared by the Defence Force in accordance with section 43(1) of the Public Finance Act 1989.

The fund’s financial statements and annual report are to be treated, for the purposes of sections 43(2), 44, and 45D of the Public Finance Act 1989, as if they are part of the Defence Force’s financial statements and annual report.

prov.heading: Investment of capital and income

para.text: Any money that is capital of the fund held by the Crown may be invested in accordance with the Trusts Act 2019.
VANZ may invest income of the fund in accordance with the Trusts Act 2019.
